Next lesson: Circle Equations and Intersections.

screens.title: Standard form reveals the centre and radius

diagram.caption: Every point on the circle is distance r from the centre (a, b).

screens.body:
The circle with centre [[(a,b)]] and radius r has equation

[[(x-a)^{2}+(y-b)^{2}=r^{2}]].

The signs inside the brackets are opposite to the centre coordinates, and the right-hand side is the radius squared.
screens.keyPoint: Read [[(x+3)^{2}]] as [[(x-(-3))^{2}]]: its centre coordinate is -3.

screens.question: What are the centre and radius of [[(x+3)^{2}+(y-2)^{2}=25]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The centre is [[(-3,2)]] and [[r=\sqrt{25}=5]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Reverse the bracket signs for the centre, then take the positive square root of the right-hand side.

screens.title: Convert expanded form into standard form
screens.body:
A circle may be written [[x^{2}+y^{2}+cx+dy+e=0]]. Group the x-terms and y-terms, then complete each square.

Use [[x^{2}+cx=\left(x+\frac c2\right)^{2}-\left(\frac c2\right)^{2}]], and similarly for y.
screens.keyPoint: Whatever constants you add to complete the squares must be balanced on both sides.

screens.title: Reveal the centre and radius
screens.question: Find the centre and radius of [[x^{2}+y^{2}-6x+4y-12=0]].

diagram.caption: Completing both squares converts the expanded equation into geometric information.

steps.title: Collect the constants
steps.body: [[(x-3)^{2}+(y+2)^{2}=25]]. The centre is [[(3,-2)]] and the radius is 5.

screens.finalAnswer: The centre is [[(3,-2)]] and the radius is 5, so the correct option is B.

screens.question: Find the centre and radius of [[x^{2}+y^{2}+8x-2y+8=0]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The equation becomes [[(x+4)^{2}+(y-1)^{2}=9]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Complete both squares: add 16 for the x-terms and 1 for the y-terms.

screens.title: You can extract geometry from either form of a circle equation.
screens.body:
You have read standard form and completed squares in expanded form.

Next, we test points, intersect circles with lines, and construct a circle from a diameter.

screens.title: Compare squared distance with [[r^{2}]]
screens.body:
For a circle centred at [[(a,b)]], calculate [[D^{2}=(x-a)^{2}+(y-b)^{2}]] for a point [[(x,y)]].

If [[D^{2}=r^{2}]], the point is on the circle. If [[D^{2}r^{2}]], it is outside.
screens.keyPoint: Use squared distances to avoid unnecessary square roots.

screens.question: Relative to the circle with centre [[(1,-2)]] and radius 5, where is the point [[(4,2)]]?
screens.incorrectFeedback: Compare the squared distance from [[(1,-2)]] with [[5^{2}]].

screens.title: Substitution produces a quadratic

diagram.caption: Two, one, or zero real quadratic roots correspond to a secant, tangent, or non-intersecting line.

screens.body:
Substitute the line equation into the circle equation. The resulting quadratic gives the intersection x-values.

A positive discriminant gives two intersections, zero gives one tangent point, and a negative discriminant gives no real intersection.
screens.keyPoint: After solving for x, substitute into the line equation to recover each y-coordinate.

screens.title: Find both line-circle intersections
screens.question: Find the intersections of [[x^{2}+y^{2}=25]] and [[y=x+1]].

diagram.caption: The line cuts the circle at two points, so the substituted quadratic has two roots.

screens.finalAnswer: The intersections are [[(3,4)]] and [[(-4,-3)]], so the correct option is A.

screens.question: Where does the line [[y=4]] meet the circle [[x^{2}+y^{2}=25]]?
options: It does not meet the circle
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Substitution gives [[x^{2}+16=25]], so [[x=\pm3]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Set [[y=4]] in the circle equation, solve for both x-values, and retain the fixed y-coordinate.

screens.title: A diameter determines the centre and radius
screens.body:
If A and B are endpoints of a diameter, the centre is their midpoint. The radius is half the distance AB.

It is often quickest to calculate [[r^{2}]] directly as one quarter of the squared diameter length.
screens.keyPoint: Once the midpoint and [[r^{2}]] are known, place them directly into standard circle form.

screens.title: Construct a circle from its diameter
screens.question: A circle has diameter endpoints [[(-2,1)]] and [[(4,5)]]. Find its equation.

steps.title: Find the squared radius
steps.body: Since the radius is half the diameter, [[r^{2}=\frac{52}{4}=13]].

screens.finalAnswer: The equation is [[(x-1)^{2}+(y-3)^{2}=13]], so the correct option is A.

screens.title: Circle Equations and Intersections: key points
keyPoints: [[(x-a)^{2}+(y-b)^{2}=r^{2}]] has centre [[(a,b)]] and radius r.
Complete the x-square and y-square separately in expanded form.
Compare squared distance with [[r^{2}]] to classify a point.
Line-circle intersections reduce to a quadratic after substitution.
Diameter endpoints give the centre by midpoint and the radius by half-distance.
reviewItems: Reverse bracket signs when reading centre coordinates.
Balance constants carefully while completing squares.
Use the quadratic discriminant to count intersections.
Recover y-values after solving intersection x-values.
Next lesson: Circle Theorems on Axes.

screens.title: A tangent is perpendicular to the radius at contact

diagram.caption: Join the point of contact to the centre before finding the tangent gradient.

screens.body:
To find a tangent equation, calculate the gradient from the centre to the contact point, then take its negative reciprocal.

The tangent passes through the contact point. If the radius is vertical, the tangent is horizontal; if the radius is horizontal, the tangent is vertical.
screens.keyPoint: First confirm that the stated contact point lies on the circle.

screens.title: Find a tangent without differentiation
screens.question: Find the tangent to [[x^{2}+y^{2}=25]] at [[(3,4)]].

steps.title: Identify the centre
steps.body: The centre is [[(0,0)]]. The point [[(3,4)]] lies on the circle because [[3^{2}+4^{2}=25]].

screens.finalAnswer: The tangent is [[3x+4y=25]], so the correct option is A.

screens.question: A circle has centre [[(2,-1)]], and [[P=(5,3)]] lies on it. Which line is tangent at P?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The radius gradient is [[\frac{3-(-1)}{5-2}=\frac43]], so the tangent gradient is [[-\frac34]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Find the centre-to-P gradient, take its negative reciprocal, and use P in point-gradient form.

screens.title: The perpendicular from the centre bisects a chord

diagram.caption: The centre lies on the perpendicular bisector of every chord.

screens.body:
For chord endpoints A and B, its midpoint M is found by averaging coordinates. The line from the centre to M is perpendicular to AB.

Therefore an unknown centre can be located by intersecting perpendicular bisectors of chords or combining one such bisector with other information.
screens.keyPoint: A chord's perpendicular bisector passes through the circle centre even when the chord is not a diameter.

screens.title: Locate a centre from a chord
screens.question: A circle contains chord endpoints [[A=(2,6)]] and [[B=(8,2)]]. Its centre lies on [[y=x]]. Find the centre.

diagram.caption: The centre is where the chord's perpendicular bisector meets y = x.

steps.title: Intersect with the known centre line
steps.body: Set [[y=x]]. Solving [[x-4=\frac32(x-5)]] gives [[x=7]], so the centre is [[(7,7)]].

screens.finalAnswer: The centre is [[(7,7)]], so the correct option is D.

screens.question: A circle has radius 5 and a chord of length 8. What is the perpendicular distance from the centre to the chord?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The perpendicular bisects the chord, giving a right triangle with hypotenuse 5 and one leg 4. The other leg is [[\sqrt{25-16}=3]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Halve the chord first, then use Pythagoras with the radius as the hypotenuse.

screens.title: You can combine gradients, midpoints, and circle structure.
screens.body:
You have constructed tangents and located centres from chords.

Next, we connect coordinate information with the remaining angle theorems.

screens.title: The angle at the centre is twice the angle at the circumference
screens.body:
For the same arc AB, [[\angle AOB=2\angle ACB]], where O is the centre and C is on the circumference.

Angles standing on the same chord in the same segment are equal. Coordinate information may first be needed to identify the centre, endpoints, or diameter.
screens.keyPoint: Check that the angles refer to the same arc or chord before applying a circle theorem.

screens.title: Translate between related angles

steps.title: Identify the common arc
steps.body: Suppose chord AB subtends [[34^{\circ}]] at a point C on the circumference.

steps.title: Use the centre theorem
steps.body: At the centre O, the same chord subtends twice that angle.

steps.title: Use the same segment
steps.body: Any other point on the same segment standing on chord AB also sees an angle of [[34^{\circ}]].

screens.question: One angle of a cyclic quadrilateral is 117°. What is the opposite angle?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Opposite angles in a cyclic quadrilateral sum to 180°, so [[180^{\circ}-117^{\circ}=63^{\circ}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the supplementary opposite-angle property of a cyclic quadrilateral.

screens.title: Diameters create right angles; tangents transfer angles
screens.body:
An angle subtended by a diameter at the circumference is 90°.

The angle between a tangent and a chord at the point of contact equals the angle made by that chord in the alternate segment. These facts can complete a triangle after coordinate work identifies a diameter or tangent.
screens.keyPoint: The radius-tangent right angle and the alternate-segment theorem are different facts; use the one matching the requested angle.

screens.title: Use a coordinate diameter
screens.question: A circle has diameter endpoints [[A=(-3,1)]] and [[B=(5,1)]]. Point C lies elsewhere on the circle, and [[\angle CAB=32^{\circ}]]. Find [[\angle ABC]].

steps.title: Recognise the diameter
steps.body: AB passes through the centre because its endpoints define the diameter.

screens.finalAnswer: The angle is [[58^{\circ}]], so the correct option is C.

screens.question: The angle between a tangent and a chord is 47°. What angle does that chord subtend in the alternate segment?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The alternate-segment theorem makes the two angles equal.
screens.incorrectFeedback: The angle between the tangent and chord equals the angle subtended by the chord in the alternate segment.

screens.title: Circle Theorems on Axes: key points
keyPoints: A tangent is perpendicular to the radius at the contact point.
The centre lies on the perpendicular bisector of every chord.
The angle at the centre is twice the angle at the circumference on the same arc.
An angle in a semicircle is 90°, and opposite cyclic angles sum to 180°.
The tangent-chord angle equals the angle in the alternate segment.
reviewItems: Confirm contact points satisfy the circle equation.
Use midpoint and negative-reciprocal gradient for chord bisectors.
Name the common arc before applying an angle theorem.
Combine coordinate facts and geometric facts in separate clear stages.
Next lesson: Coordinate Geometry Exam-style Questions.

screens.question: Find the gradient of the line through [[(-3,4)]] and [[(5,-8)]].

diagram.caption: Use a consistent coordinate subtraction order.

screens.incorrectFeedback: Calculate second y minus first y over second x minus first x.

screens.question: Find the equation of the line through [[(2,5)]] with gradient -3.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use point-gradient form and expand carefully.

screens.question: What is the gradient of [[5x+y-7=0]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Rearranging gives [[y=-5x+7]], so the gradient is -5.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Make y the subject before reading the coefficient of x.

screens.question: Which line passes through [[(2,1)]] and is parallel to [[3x-y=4]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The gradient is 3. Through [[(2,1)]], [[y-1=3(x-2)]], giving [[3x-y-5=0]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the same gradient as the given line, then impose the new point.

screens.question: Which line passes through [[(0,-3)]] and is perpendicular to [[2x+5y=1]]?

diagram.caption: Perpendicular non-vertical gradients are negative reciprocals.

screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The original gradient is [[-\frac25]], so the perpendicular gradient is [[\frac52]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Rearrange the original line, then take the negative reciprocal of its gradient.

screens.question: Find the midpoint of the segment joining [[(-4,2)]] and [[(6,8)]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Average corresponding coordinates: [[\left(\frac{-4+6}{2},\frac{2+8}{2}\right)=(1,5)]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: A midpoint is the coordinate-wise average of the endpoints.

screens.question: Find the perpendicular bisector of the segment joining [[(0,0)]] and [[(4,2)]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The midpoint is [[(2,1)]], the segment gradient is [[\frac12]], and the perpendicular gradient is -2. Hence [[y-1=-2(x-2)]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Find both the midpoint and the negative-reciprocal gradient before forming the line.

screens.question: What are the centre and radius of [[(x-4)^{2}+(y+1)^{2}=36]]?

diagram.caption: Standard form displays the centre and squared radius.

screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The centre is [[(4,-1)]] and [[r=\sqrt{36}=6]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Reverse the signs inside the brackets and take the square root of the right side.

screens.question: Find the centre and radius of [[x^{2}+y^{2}-4x+6y-12=0]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Completing squares gives [[(x-2)^{2}+(y+3)^{2}=25]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Complete the x-square and y-square separately, then collect the constants.

screens.question: Where is [[P=(4,5)]] relative to [[(x-1)^{2}+(y-1)^{2}=16]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The squared centre-to-P distance is [[3^{2}+4^{2}=25]], which is greater than [[r^{2}=16]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Compare the squared distance from [[(1,1)]] with 16.

screens.question: How many points of intersection do [[x^{2}+y^{2}=25]] and [[y=5]] have?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Substitution gives [[x^{2}+25=25]], so [[x=0]]. The line is tangent at [[(0,5)]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Substitute [[y=5]] into the circle and count the real x-values.

screens.question: Where does [[y=0]] meet [[(x-2)^{2}+(y+1)^{2}=10]]?

diagram.caption: Substitute the line equation and solve for both points.

screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Setting [[y=0]] gives [[(x-2)^{2}+1=10]], so [[x-2=\pm3]] and [[x=5]] or [[x=-1]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Substitute y = 0, isolate the squared bracket, and remember both square roots.

screens.question: A circle has diameter endpoints [[(0,0)]] and [[(6,8)]]. Which is its equation?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The centre is [[(3,4)]], the diameter is 10, and the radius is 5.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the diameter midpoint for the centre and half the endpoint distance for the radius.

screens.question: Find the tangent to [[x^{2}+y^{2}=13]] at [[(2,3)]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The radius gradient is [[\frac32]], so the tangent gradient is [[-\frac23]]. Point-gradient form rearranges to [[2x+3y=13]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Find the origin-to-point radius gradient, take its negative reciprocal, and use [[(2,3)]].

screens.question: A circle has centre [[(1,-1)]], and [[P=(4,3)]] lies on it. What is the tangent gradient at P?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The radius gradient is [[\frac{3-(-1)}{4-1}=\frac43]], so the tangent gradient is [[-\frac34]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Calculate the centre-to-P gradient and take its negative reciprocal.

screens.question: A circle has radius 5 and a chord of length 6. Find the perpendicular distance from the centre to the chord.

diagram.caption: The perpendicular from the centre bisects the chord.

screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Half the chord is 3, so the distance is [[\sqrt{5^{2}-3^{2}}=4]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Halve the chord, then use a right triangle with radius 5 as the hypotenuse.

screens.question: A chord subtends an angle of 42° at the circumference. What angle does it subtend at the centre?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The angle at the centre is twice the angle at the circumference: [[2(42^{\circ})=84^{\circ}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Double the circumference angle for the same chord and arc.

screens.question: Two points on the same segment of a circle stand on the same chord. One subtended angle is 31°. What is the other?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Angles in the same segment standing on the same chord are equal.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the same-segment theorem rather than doubling or supplementing the angle.

screens.question: One angle of a cyclic quadrilateral is 128°. Find the opposite angle.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Opposite cyclic angles total 180°, so [[180^{\circ}-128^{\circ}=52^{\circ}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Subtract the given angle from 180°.

screens.question: AB is a diameter of a circle and C lies on the circumference. If [[\angle CAB=27^{\circ}]], find [[\angle ABC]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[\angle ACB=90^{\circ}]] because AB is a diameter. Thus [[\angle ABC=180^{\circ}-90^{\circ}-27^{\circ}=63^{\circ}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the angle-in-a-semicircle theorem, then the triangle angle sum.

screens.title: Coordinate Geometry: exam review
keyPoints: Use gradients, point-gradient form, and midpoint or distance formulae systematically.
Parallel lines share gradients; perpendicular gradients are negative reciprocals.
Convert expanded circles by completing both squares.
Substitute line equations into circles and interpret the quadratic roots geometrically.
Combine coordinate calculations with tangent, chord, and angle theorems.
reviewItems: Check signs when reading circle centres.
Treat vertical and horizontal lines explicitly.
Retain both roots when a line crosses a circle twice.
Verify contact points before constructing tangents.
Repeat this set until algebraic and geometric information connect immediately.

screens.title: Match each side with its opposite angle
screens.body:
In any triangle, lower-case side [[a]] lies opposite angle [[A]], and similarly for [[b,B]] and [[c,C]].

The sine rule is [[\frac{a}{\sin A}=\frac{b}{\sin B}=\frac{c}{\sin C}]]. The cosine rule is [[c^{2}=a^{2}+b^{2}-2ab\cos C]].
screens.keyPoint: Use the area formula [[\text{Area}=\frac12 ab\sin C]] only when [[C]] is the included angle between sides [[a]] and [[b]].

diagram.caption: A side and its matching capital angle are opposite one another.

screens.title: Let the given information choose the method

steps.title: Look for an opposite pair
steps.body: If you know a side and its opposite angle, and need another side or angle, start with the sine rule.

steps.title: Three sides or an included angle
steps.body: Use the cosine rule for [[\text{SSS}]] or [[\text{SAS}]] information. Rearranged, [[\cos C=\frac{a^{2}+b^{2}-c^{2}}{2ab}]].

steps.title: Two sides and the included angle
steps.body: For area, use [[\frac12 ab\sin C]]. If the known angle is not between the two known sides, relabel or find more information first.

screens.question: Two sides of a triangle have lengths [[5]] and [[7]], and their included angle is [[60^{\circ}]]. Find the third side.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the cosine rule and remember that [[\cos60^{\circ}=\frac12]].

screens.title: One sine value can produce two angles
screens.question: In triangle [[ABC]], [[A=35^{\circ}]], [[a=8]], and [[b=11]]. Find the possible values of [[B]] to one decimal place.

diagram.caption: With angle-side-side information, the moving side can sometimes meet the ray twice.

steps.title: Test the supplementary angle
steps.body: Because [[\sin(180^{\circ}-B)=\sin B]], the other candidate is [[127.9^{\circ}]]. Both work because [[35^{\circ}+127.9^{\circ}\lt180^{\circ}]].

screens.finalAnswer: [[B\approx52.1^{\circ}]] or [[B\approx127.9^{\circ}]]

screens.question: Find the area of a triangle with sides [[8]] and [[11]] enclosing an angle of [[30^{\circ}]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[\text{Area}=\frac12(8)(11)\sin30^{\circ}=44\left(\frac12\right)=22]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use [[\frac12 ab\sin C]] with the angle enclosed by the two given sides.

screens.title: A 3D problem is usually a sequence of 2D triangles
screens.body:
Mark the required length or angle, then identify the plane containing it. A diagonal across a face can become one side of a second right triangle.

For a cuboid with side lengths [[p,q,r]], the space diagonal satisfies [[d^{2}=p^{2}+q^{2}+r^{2}]].
screens.keyPoint: Never apply a triangle rule to lengths that do not lie in the same plane. Build the relevant face or cross-section first.

screens.title: Use a face diagonal as the bridge
screens.question: A cuboid has base dimensions [[3]] by [[4]] and height [[12]]. If [[\theta]] is the angle between the space diagonal and the base, find [[\cos\theta]].

steps.title: Find the projection on the base
Find the space diagonal
steps.body: The base diagonal and height form a right triangle, so [[d=\sqrt{5^{2}+12^{2}}=13]].

steps.title: Use the requested ratio
steps.body: Relative to [[\theta]], the adjacent side is the base diagonal. Therefore [[\cos\theta=\frac5{13}]].

screens.finalAnswer: [[\cos\theta=\frac5{13}]]

screens.title: You can now choose and combine triangle methods.
screens.body: Next, angles become lengths and areas through radian measure.

screens.title: Radians measure angle by arc length
screens.body:
An angle of one radian subtends an arc equal in length to the radius. Since a full circumference is [[2\pi r]], a full turn is [[2\pi]] radians.

Therefore [[180^{\circ}=\pi\text{ radians}]], so multiply degrees by [[\frac{\pi}{180}]] and radians by [[\frac{180}{\pi}]].
screens.keyPoint: The formulae [[s=r\theta]] and [[A=\frac12r^{2}\theta]] require [[\theta]] in radians.

diagram.caption: Radian measure makes arc length directly proportional to radius.

screens.question: Convert [[150^{\circ}]] to radians.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[150\times\frac{\pi}{180}=\frac{5\pi}{6}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Multiply the degree measure by [[\frac{\pi}{180}]] and simplify.

screens.title: Arc, sector, then segment

diagram.caption: A segment is the sector minus the triangle formed by the two radii.

steps.title: Sector area
steps.body: The sector is the fraction [[\frac{\theta}{2\pi}]] of a circle, giving [[A=\frac12r^{2}\theta]].

screens.question: A sector has radius [[6]] and angle [[\frac23]] radians. What are its arc length and area?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[s=6\left(\frac23\right)=4]] and [[A=\frac12(6^{2})\left(\frac23\right)=12]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use [[s=r\theta]] and [[A=\frac12r^{2}\theta]] with [[\theta]] already in radians.

screens.title: Subtract the central triangle
screens.question: Find the area of the minor segment in a circle of radius [[4]] subtended by an angle of [[\frac{\pi}{3}]] radians.

steps.title: Subtract
steps.body: The minor segment is the sector minus the triangle.

screens.title: Triangles and Radians: key points
keyPoints: Use opposite side-angle pairs consistently in the sine rule.
Use the cosine rule for [[\text{SSS}]] or [[\text{SAS}]] information.
Check the supplementary angle in the ambiguous sine-rule case.
Reduce 3D problems to triangles lying in clearly identified planes.
For radians, remember [[s=r\theta]] and [[A=\frac12r^{2}\theta]].
reviewItems: Label sides before choosing a rule.
Check whether a given angle is included or opposite a known side.
Use exact values until a decimal answer is explicitly required.
Confirm that sector formulae use radians.
For a segment, subtract the central triangle from the sector.

screens.title: Exact values come from two special triangles
screens.body:
A [[45^{\circ}]] right triangle has side ratio [[1:1:\sqrt2]]. A [[30^{\circ}\text{-}60^{\circ}]] right triangle has ratio [[1:\sqrt3:2]].

On the unit circle, the point at angle [[\theta]] is [[(\cos\theta,\sin\theta)]]. These two ideas generate every required exact value.
screens.keyPoint: Keep answers such as [[\frac{\sqrt3}{2}]] exact. Do not replace them with rounded decimals.

diagram.caption: Cosine is the horizontal coordinate; sine is the vertical coordinate.

screens.title: Use one pattern instead of memorising fifteen facts

steps.title: Cosine reverses the pattern
steps.body: For the same angles, cosine runs from [[\frac{\sqrt4}{2}]] down to [[\frac{\sqrt0}{2}]].

steps.title: Tangent is a quotient
steps.body: Use [[\tan\theta=\frac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta}]]. This gives [[0,\frac1{\sqrt3},1,\sqrt3]], while [[\tan90^{\circ}]] is undefined.

screens.question: Evaluate [[\sin60^{\circ}\cos45^{\circ}]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[\sin60^{\circ}\cos45^{\circ}=\frac{\sqrt3}{2}\cdot\frac{\sqrt2}{2}=\frac{\sqrt6}{4}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Substitute the two exact values, then multiply the numerators and denominators.

screens.title: The reference angle gives size; the quadrant gives sign
screens.body:
On the unit circle, [[\sin\theta]] is the y-coordinate and [[\cos\theta]] is the x-coordinate. Tangent is their quotient.

Sine is positive in quadrants I and II. Cosine is positive in I and IV. Tangent is positive in I and III.
screens.keyPoint: First reduce to an acute reference angle. Then attach the sign required by the quadrant.

diagram.caption: Coordinates explain both exact values and their signs.

screens.question: Evaluate [[\sin150^{\circ}]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The reference angle is [[30^{\circ}]], and sine is positive in quadrant II, so [[\sin150^{\circ}=\frac12]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use reference angle [[180^{\circ}-150^{\circ}=30^{\circ}]] and determine the sign from quadrant II.

screens.title: Periodicity repeats the same geometry
screens.body:
The sine and cosine graphs have period [[2\pi]]: [[f(x+2\pi)=f(x)]]. The tangent graph has period [[\pi]].

Both sine and cosine range from [[-1]] to [[1]]. Tangent has zeros at [[x=k\pi]] and is undefined at [[x=\frac{\pi}{2}+k\pi]].
screens.keyPoint: Sine is odd: [[\sin(-x)=-\sin x]]. Cosine is even: [[\cos(-x)=\cos x]].

diagram.caption: Sine starts at zero; cosine starts at its maximum.

screens.title: Read multipliers from the inside out

steps.title: Vertical multiplier
steps.body: For [[y=a\sin x]] or [[y=a\cos x]], the amplitude is [[\lvert a\rvert]] and the range satisfies [[-\lvert a\rvert\le y\le\lvert a\rvert]].

steps.title: Horizontal multiplier
steps.body: The period of [[\sin(bx)]] and [[\cos(bx)]] is [[\frac{2\pi}{\lvert b\rvert}]]. The period of [[\tan(bx)]] is [[\frac{\pi}{\lvert b\rvert}]].

steps.title: A shift changes location, not period
steps.body: Replacing [[x]] by [[x-c]] shifts the graph right by [[c]]. Its amplitude and period stay unchanged.

screens.question: What is the period of [[y=\tan(3x)]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Tangent's base period is [[\pi]], so the period becomes [[\frac{\pi}{3}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: For [[\tan(bx)]], divide the base period [[\pi]] by [[\lvert b\rvert]].

screens.title: You can connect triangles, circles, and graphs.
screens.body: The final step is using identities to change one trigonometric form into another.

screens.title: Two identities do most of the work
screens.body:
The quotient identity is [[\tan\theta=\frac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta}]]. The Pythagorean identity is [[\sin^{2}\theta+\cos^{2}\theta=1]].

Useful rearrangements include [[\sin^{2}\theta=1-\cos^{2}\theta]] and [[\cos^{2}\theta=1-\sin^{2}\theta]].
screens.keyPoint: Choose the rearrangement that leaves the entire expression in one trigonometric function.

screens.title: Replace the numerator before cancelling
screens.question: Simplify [[\frac{1-\sin^{2}x}{\cos x}]], where [[\cos x\ne0]].

steps.title: Cancel within the stated domain
steps.body: Since [[\cos x\ne0]], the quotient simplifies to [[\cos x]].

screens.finalAnswer: [[\cos x]]

screens.question: An acute angle [[\theta]] satisfies [[\tan\theta=\frac34]]. Find [[\sin\theta]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Use a right triangle with opposite [[3]], adjacent [[4]], and hypotenuse [[5]]. Thus [[\sin\theta=\frac35]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Interpret tangent as opposite over adjacent, then complete the right triangle.

screens.question: Which statement is true for every real [[x]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Cosine is even, so [[\cos(-x)=\cos x]]. Sine and tangent are odd.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Recall the mirror symmetry of the cosine graph about the y-axis.

screens.title: Values, Graphs, and Identities: key points
keyPoints: Generate exact values from the two special right triangles.
Use the reference angle for magnitude and the quadrant for sign.
Sine and cosine have period [[2\pi]]; tangent has period [[\pi]].
Cosine is even, while sine and tangent are odd.
Use [[\tan x=\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}]] and [[\sin^{2}x+\cos^{2}x=1]].
reviewItems: Keep exact values exact.
Distinguish amplitude from period.
Check where tangent is undefined.
Use graph symmetry to evaluate negative angles.
Convert identities into the single function you need.

screens.title: Solve the value, locate the quadrants, filter the interval
screens.body:
For an equation such as [[\sin x=k]], first find the reference angle. Then use the sign of the function to select every valid quadrant.

Finally, use periodicity to list all solutions in the stated interval. The interval is part of the question, not an afterthought.
screens.keyPoint: Never stop after the first inverse-trigonometric value. A single function value usually occurs more than once in a full cycle.

diagram.caption: A positive sine value appears in quadrants I and II.

screens.title: Solve [[\sin x=\frac12]] for [[0\le x\lt2\pi]]

steps.title: Choose the quadrants
steps.body: Sine is positive in quadrants I and II.

diagram.caption: The horizontal coordinate changes sign, but the sine value stays the same.

screens.question: Solve [[\cos x=-\frac{\sqrt2}{2}]] for [[0\le x\lt2\pi]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The reference angle is [[\frac{\pi}{4}]], and cosine is negative in quadrants II and III.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use reference angle [[\frac{\pi}{4}]], then choose the two quadrants where the x-coordinate is negative.

screens.title: Tangent repeats every [[\pi]]
screens.body:
If [[\tan x=k]] has one solution [[x=\alpha]], then its general solution is [[x=\alpha+n\pi]], where [[n]] is an integer.

This single family captures the solutions in quadrants I and III, or II and IV, because opposite points on the unit circle have the same tangent ratio.
screens.keyPoint: When the argument is [[bx+c]], solve for the whole angle first, then isolate [[x]].

screens.title: Transform the interval with the angle
screens.question: Solve [[\tan(2x)=-1]] for [[-\frac{\pi}{2}\lt x\lt\frac{\pi}{2}]].

steps.title: Solve for the new angle
steps.body: Within this interval, [[\tan u=-1]] at [[u=-\frac{\pi}{4}]] and [[u=\frac{3\pi}{4}]].

steps.title: Return to x
steps.body: Divide both values by [[2]] to obtain [[x=-\frac{\pi}{8}]] and [[x=\frac{3\pi}{8}]].

screens.question: How many solutions does [[\cos(2x)=0]] have in [[0\le x\lt2\pi]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[2x=\frac{\pi}{2},\frac{3\pi}{2},\frac{5\pi}{2},\frac{7\pi}{2}]], giving four values of [[x]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Transform the interval to [[0\le2x\lt4\pi]] and count every cosine zero.

screens.title: You can solve direct equations over any interval.
screens.body: Next, identities turn disguised equations into algebraic ones.

screens.title: Treat one trigonometric function as the variable
screens.body:
An equation such as [[2\sin^{2}x-\sin x-1=0]] is a quadratic in [[\sin x]]. Factor or solve it exactly, then solve each resulting trigonometric equation.

If both sine and cosine appear, use [[\sin^{2}x+\cos^{2}x=1]] to rewrite everything in one function.
screens.keyPoint: Reject algebraic roots outside [[-1\le\sin x\le1]] or [[-1\le\cos x\le1]].

screens.title: Convert cosine squared into sine
screens.question: Solve [[2\cos^{2}x-3\sin x=0]] for [[0^{\circ}\le x\lt360^{\circ}]].

steps.title: Factor the quadratic
steps.body: Rearranging gives [[2\sin^{2}x+3\sin x-2=0]], so [[(2\sin x-1)(\sin x+2)=0]].

steps.title: Keep valid function values
steps.body: [[\sin x=-2]] is impossible. From [[\sin x=\frac12]], the interval gives [[x=30^{\circ},150^{\circ}]].

screens.question: Solve [[2\sin^{2}x-\sin x-1=0]] for [[0^{\circ}\le x\lt360^{\circ}]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[(2\sin x+1)(\sin x-1)=0]], so [[\sin x=-\frac12]] or [[\sin x=1]]. The interval gives [[90^{\circ},210^{\circ},330^{\circ}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Factor as a quadratic in [[\sin x]], then solve both valid sine equations.

screens.title: Endpoints and transformed intervals decide the final list
screens.body:
Distinguish [[a\lt x\lt b]] from [[a\le x\le b]]. An endpoint may duplicate a periodic value, but it is included only when the inequality allows it.

For [[f(bx+c)=k]], either transform the interval for [[u=bx+c]] or generate a general solution before filtering.
screens.keyPoint: Substitute each final candidate into the original equation, especially after squaring, dividing, or changing variables.

screens.title: Preserve every valid branch
screens.question: Solve [[12\cos^{2}x+6\sin x-10=2]] for [[0^{\circ}\lt x\lt360^{\circ}]].

steps.title: Filter the open interval
steps.body: [[\sin x=0]] gives [[180^{\circ}]] because [[0^{\circ}]] and [[360^{\circ}]] are excluded. [[\sin x=\frac12]] gives [[30^{\circ},150^{\circ}]].

screens.question: How many solutions does [[\sin^{2}\left(2x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)=\frac12]] have for [[-2\pi\lt x\lt2\pi]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[\sin^{2}u=\frac12]] repeats every [[\frac{\pi}{2}]] in [[u]]. With [[u=2x+\frac{\pi}{3}]], the solutions in the transformed interval correspond to sixteen integer values.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Write [[u=\frac{\pi}{4}+k\frac{\pi}{2}]], substitute [[u=2x+\frac{\pi}{3}]], then count integers satisfying the x-interval.

screens.title: Solving Trigonometric Equations: key points
keyPoints: Find a reference angle, choose quadrants, then filter the interval.
Use periods [[2\pi]] for sine and cosine and [[\pi]] for tangent.
Transform the interval when the argument is [[bx+c]].
Treat equations in [[\sin x]] or [[\cos x]] as ordinary quadratics.
Use [[\sin^{2}x+\cos^{2}x=1]] to reduce mixed equations to one function.
reviewItems: List every solution, not only the inverse-function output.
Respect open and closed endpoints.
Reject impossible sine or cosine values.
Check solutions in the original equation.
Count periodic solutions algebraically on long intervals.

screens.question: Two sides of a triangle have lengths [[7]] and [[8]], and their included angle is [[60^{\circ}]]. Find the third side.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the cosine rule with [[\cos60^{\circ}=\frac12]].

screens.question: A triangle has sides [[5]], [[7]], and [[8]]. Find the cosine of the angle opposite the side of length [[8]].

diagram.caption: For an unknown angle, rearrange the cosine rule.

screens.incorrectFeedback: Place the side of length [[8]] opposite the required angle in the rearranged cosine rule.

screens.question: In triangle [[ABC]], [[A=30^{\circ}]], [[B=45^{\circ}]], and [[b=6\sqrt2]]. Find [[a]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[\frac{a}{\sin30^{\circ}}=\frac{6\sqrt2}{\sin45^{\circ}}=12]], so [[a=12\left(\frac12\right)=6]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the two known opposite pairs in the sine rule.

screens.question: In triangle [[ABC]], [[A=30^{\circ}]], [[a=5]], and [[b=8]]. How many different triangles satisfy the information?

diagram.caption: Check both the acute and supplementary sine-rule angles.

screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[\sin B=\frac{8\sin30^{\circ}}5=\frac45]]. Both [[B]] and [[180^{\circ}-B]] leave a positive third angle, so two triangles exist.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Find [[\sin B]], then test both angles with that sine value against the triangle angle sum.

screens.question: Find the area of a triangle with sides [[9]] and [[12]] enclosing an angle of [[30^{\circ}]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[\frac12(9)(12)\sin30^{\circ}=54\left(\frac12\right)=27]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the included-angle area formula.

screens.question: A cuboid has side lengths [[2]], [[3]], and [[6]]. Find its space diagonal.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Apply Pythagoras across a face and then through the cuboid, or combine all three squared dimensions.

screens.question: Convert [[225^{\circ}]] to radians.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[225\times\frac{\pi}{180}=\frac{5\pi}{4}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Multiply by [[\frac{\pi}{180}]] and simplify.

screens.question: Convert [[\frac{7\pi}{6}]] radians to degrees.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[\frac{7\pi}{6}\times\frac{180}{\pi}=210^{\circ}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Multiply by [[\frac{180}{\pi}]].

screens.question: An arc has radius [[9]] and subtends [[\frac23]] radians at the centre. Find its length.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[s=r\theta=9\left(\frac23\right)=6]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use [[s=r\theta]] because the angle is already in radians.

screens.question: A sector has radius [[5]] and angle [[1.2]] radians. Find its area.

diagram.caption: Sector formulae use the central angle in radians.

screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[A=\frac12r^{2}\theta=\frac12(25)(1.2)=15]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Substitute into [[A=\frac12r^{2}\theta]].

screens.question: A minor segment has radius [[2]] and central angle [[\frac{\pi}{2}]]. Find its area.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The sector area is [[\pi]], and the right-triangle area is [[2]], so the segment area is [[\pi-2]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Subtract [[\frac12r^{2}\sin\theta]] from [[\frac12r^{2}\theta]].

screens.question: Evaluate [[\sin30^{\circ}+\cos60^{\circ}]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Both exact values equal [[\frac12]], so their sum is [[1]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Recall the exact sine of [[30^{\circ}]] and cosine of [[60^{\circ}]].

screens.question: Evaluate [[\tan45^{\circ}\cos60^{\circ}]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[\tan45^{\circ}=1]] and [[\cos60^{\circ}=\frac12]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Substitute both exact values before multiplying.

screens.question: Evaluate [[\sin\left(\frac{5\pi}{6}\right)]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The reference angle is [[\frac{\pi}{6}]], and sine is positive in quadrant II.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the reference angle and the sign of sine in quadrant II.

screens.question: What is the period of [[y=\cos(3x)]]?

diagram.caption: A horizontal multiplier compresses the graph's period.

screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The period is [[\frac{2\pi}{3}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Divide cosine's base period [[2\pi]] by the coefficient of [[x]].

screens.question: An angle [[\theta]] lies in quadrant III and [[\tan\theta=\frac34]]. Find [[\cos\theta]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The reference triangle has sides [[3,4,5]]. Cosine has magnitude [[\frac45]] and is negative in quadrant III.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Build a [[3\text{-}4\text{-}5]] reference triangle, then apply the quadrant III sign.

screens.question: Solve [[\sin x=-\frac12]] for [[0\le x\lt2\pi]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The reference angle is [[\frac{\pi}{6}]], and sine is negative in quadrants III and IV.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use reference angle [[\frac{\pi}{6}]] and select the two quadrants below the x-axis.

screens.question: Solve [[\cos(2x)=0]] for [[0\le x\lt2\pi]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. In [[0\le2x\lt4\pi]], cosine is zero at four odd multiples of [[\frac{\pi}{2}]]. Divide each by [[2]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Transform the interval for [[2x]], list every cosine zero, then divide by [[2]].

screens.question: Solve [[\tan x=1]] for [[-\pi\lt x\lt\pi]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[x=\frac{\pi}{4}+n\pi]], giving [[-\frac{3\pi}{4}]] and [[\frac{\pi}{4}]] in the interval.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Start at [[\frac{\pi}{4}]] and move by tangent's period [[\pi]] in both directions.

screens.question: How many solutions does [[2\sin^{2}x-3\sin x+1=0]] have for [[0\le x\lt2\pi]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[(2\sin x-1)(\sin x-1)=0]], so [[\sin x=\frac12]] gives two solutions and [[\sin x=1]] gives one, for three in total.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Factor as a quadratic in [[\sin x]], then count the solutions from both valid sine values.

screens.title: Trigonometry: exam review
keyPoints: Choose sine rule, cosine rule, or the area formula from the information given.
Check the supplementary solution in angle-side-side triangle problems.
Use radians for arc length, sector area, and segment area.
Connect exact values, quadrants, graph symmetry, and periodicity.
Solve transformed and quadratic trigonometric equations over the full interval.
reviewItems: Label opposite side-angle pairs before calculating.
Keep exact values instead of rounding.
Check whether interval endpoints are included.
Reject sine or cosine values outside [[-1\le y\le1]].
Repeat the set until every periodic solution is found systematically.

screens.title: A logarithm is an exponent
screens.body:
The statements [[a^{b}=c]] and [[\log_{a}c=b]] say exactly the same thing.

For example, [[2^{5}=32]], so [[\log_{2}32=5]]. A logarithm answers the question: what power of the base produces this number?
screens.keyPoint: For real logarithms, the base must satisfy [[a>0]] and [[a\ne1]], while the logarithm's argument must be positive.

screens.title: Move confidently between exponential and logarithmic form

steps.title: Start with an exponential statement
steps.body: [[3^{4}=81]] means that the power of 3 required to make 81 is 4.

screens.question: What is [[\log_{4}64]]?
screens.prompt: Ask which power of 4 gives 64.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Rewrite the question as [[4^{x}=64]]. Since [[4^{3}=64]], the logarithm is 3.

screens.title: Every exponential graph passes through [[(0,1)]]
screens.body:
For [[y=a^{x}]], substituting [[x=0]] gives [[y=a^{0}=1]]. The graph never touches the x-axis, because [[a^{x}>0]] for every real x.

If [[a>1]], the graph increases. If [[0
screens.keyPoint: The x-axis is a horizontal asymptote. Exponential outputs are positive, so the range is [[y>0]].

screens.title: Read transformations from the equation

steps.title: Translate vertically
steps.body: [[y=2^{x}+3]] is shifted up by 3, so its horizontal asymptote is [[y=3]].

steps.title: Translate horizontally
steps.body: [[y=2^{x-1}]] is shifted one unit to the right.

screens.question: Which statement is true about [[y=\left(\frac13\right)^{x}]]?
options: It decreases and crosses the x-axis.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The base lies between 0 and 1, so the graph decreases. At [[x=0]], its value is 1.
screens.incorrectFeedback: A positive exponential never crosses the x-axis. Because [[0<\frac13<1]], the graph decreases and passes through [[(0,1)]].

screens.title: Exponential and logarithmic forms connected.
screens.body:
You can now translate between powers and logarithms and read the main features of an exponential graph.

Next, we will combine logarithms using three laws.

screens.title: Products add, quotients subtract, and powers come down
screens.body:
For positive x and y:

[[\log_{a}x+\log_{a}y=\log_{a}(xy)]].
[[\log_{a}x-\log_{a}y=\log_{a}\left(\frac{x}{y}\right)]].
[[k\log_{a}x=\log_{a}(x^{k})]].
screens.keyPoint: These laws require the same base. There is no corresponding law that turns [[\log_{a}x+\log_{b}y]] into one logarithm when the bases differ.

screens.title: Condense before calculating

screens.question: Which single logarithm is equal to [[2\log_{3}5+\log_{3}4]]?
screens.incorrectFeedback: Bring the 2 down as a power first: [[2\log_{3}5=\log_{3}(5^{2})]]. Then use the product law.

screens.question: For which values of x is [[\log_{2}(x-1)+\log_{2}(5-x)]] defined?
screens.prompt: Both logarithm arguments must be positive.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. We need [[x-1>0]] and [[5-x>0]], giving [[x>1]] and [[x<5]]. Together, [[1<x<5]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Apply the domain condition separately to both arguments: [[x-1>0]] and [[5-x>0]]. Their intersection is [[1<x<5]].

screens.title: Log Laws and Exponential Graphs: key points
keyPoints: [[a^{b}=c]] is equivalent to [[\log_{a}c=b]].
For real logarithms, [[a>0]], [[a\ne1]], and the argument must be positive.
[[y=a^{x}]] passes through [[(0,1)]] and has horizontal asymptote [[y=0]].
A base greater than 1 gives an increasing exponential; a base between 0 and 1 gives a decreasing exponential.
Logarithms of products add, logarithms of quotients subtract, and powers become coefficients.
reviewItems: Practise switching between exponential and logarithmic form.
Practise identifying exponential graph transformations.
Practise condensing several logarithms into one.
Check the domain before manipulating any logarithmic expression.
Next lesson: Solving Exponential and Log Equations.

screens.title: Write both sides using the same base
screens.body:
When each side can be expressed as a power of the same positive base, equal powers have equal exponents.

For example, [[8^{x}=4]] becomes [[2^{3x}=2^{2}]], so [[3x=2]] and [[x=\frac{2}{3}]].
screens.keyPoint: Before trying a more complicated method, look for a common base. Useful relationships include [[4=2^{2}]], [[8=2^{3}]], [[9=3^{2}]], and [[27=3^{3}]].

steps.title: Rewrite both bases as powers of 2

screens.question: Solve [[9^{x}=27]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Write both numbers as powers of 3: [[9^{x}=3^{2x}]] and [[27=3^{3}]]. Then equate the exponents.

screens.title: A repeated exponential can hide a quadratic
screens.body:
An equation containing [[a^{2x}]] and [[a^{x}]] may be quadratic in disguise because [[a^{2x}=(a^{x})^{2}]].

Set [[u=a^{x}]]. Solve the resulting quadratic, then substitute back. Remember that [[u>0]] because an exponential is always positive.
screens.keyPoint: Reject any non-positive value of u before converting back to x.

screens.title: Use substitution to expose the quadratic
screens.question: Solve [[25^{x}-6(5^{x})+5=0]].

steps.title: Solve the quadratic
steps.body: The equation becomes [[u^{2}-6u+5=0]], which factorises to [[(u-1)(u-5)=0]].

steps.title: Find the possible values of u
steps.body: Therefore [[u=1]] or [[u=5]]. Both are positive, so neither must be rejected.

screens.finalAnswer: The solutions are [[x=0]] and [[x=1]], so the correct option is C.

screens.question: Solve [[4^{x}-5(2^{x})+4=0]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use [[u=2^{x}]], so [[4^{x}=u^{2}]]. Solve [[u^{2}-5u+4=0]], then substitute back.

screens.title: You can now solve two major exponential forms.
screens.body:
You have solved equations by matching bases and by revealing a hidden quadratic.

Next, we will solve logarithmic equations while carefully checking their domains.

screens.title: Combine logarithms, solve, then check
screens.body:
Use log laws to condense an equation before converting it to exponential form.

Every logarithm argument must be positive. Algebra may produce values that make an original argument zero or negative, so every candidate solution must be checked in the original equation.
screens.keyPoint: A solution of the rearranged algebra is not automatically a solution of the logarithmic equation.

steps.title: Apply the domain
steps.body: Only [[x=5]] satisfies [[x>3]]. Therefore the solution is [[x=5]].

screens.question: Solve [[\log_{3}(x+1)-\log_{3}(x-1)=1]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The domain is [[x>1]]. The quotient law gives [[\log_{3}\left(\frac{x+1}{x-1}\right)=1]], so [[\frac{x+1}{x-1}=3]] and [[x=2]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: First note [[x>1]]. Combine the logarithms using the quotient law, then set the resulting argument equal to [[3^{1}]].

screens.question: Solve [[2\log_{5}x=\log_{5}81]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[2\log_{5}x=\log_{5}(x^{2})]], so [[x^{2}=81]]. Although the algebra gives [[x=\pm9]], the logarithm requires [[x>0]], leaving [[x=9]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the power law to get [[\log_{5}(x^{2})=\log_{5}81]]. Then solve [[x^{2}=81]] and enforce the original condition [[x>0]].

screens.title: Solving Exponential and Log Equations: key points
keyPoints: If both sides are powers of the same base, equate their exponents.
For equations involving [[a^{2x}]] and [[a^{x}]], try [[u=a^{x}]].
A substituted exponential value must be positive.
Use log laws to combine logarithms before converting to exponential form.
Check every logarithmic solution against the domain of the original equation.
reviewItems: Practise spotting common bases quickly.
Practise quadratic substitution with repeated powers.
Write domain restrictions before solving log equations.
Check candidate answers in the original equation.
Next lesson: Advanced Log Reasoning.

screens.title: The base controls the direction
screens.body:
For [[a>1]], the function [[a^{x}]] is increasing, so comparing outputs preserves the order of the exponents.

For [[0

screens.title: Compare powers with care

steps.title: An increasing base
steps.body: To solve [[2^{x+1}>8]], write [[8=2^{3}]]. Since [[2>1]], the order is preserved: [[x+1>3]], so [[x>2]].

steps.title: Reverse the comparison
steps.body: Because [[0<\frac13<1]], the exponential function decreases. Therefore [[2x>2]], so [[x>1]].

steps.title: Check the sense of the answer
steps.body: Larger exponents make powers of [[\frac13]] smaller, so [[x>1]] is consistent with the original inequality.

screens.question: Solve [[\left(\frac12\right)^{x-1}\ge4]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[4=\left(\frac12\right)^{-2}]]. Since the base lies between 0 and 1, reverse the exponent comparison: [[x-1\le-2]], so [[x\le-1]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Write [[4=\left(\frac12\right)^{-2}]]. The base is decreasing, so the comparison between the exponents is reversed.

screens.title: Translate given logarithms into powers
screens.body:
A condition such as [[\log_{a}b=2]] tells you that [[b=a^{2}]]. You can then simplify related logarithms using exponent laws or log laws.

This often avoids numerical approximation entirely.
screens.keyPoint: Keep the given relationship symbolic. TMUA questions can usually be solved using the definition and laws of logarithms alone.

screens.title: Use [[\log_{a}b=2]] to find [[\log_{a}\left(\frac{a^{3}}{b}\right)]]

screens.question: Given [[\log_{a}b=3]], find [[\log_{a}\left(\frac{b^{2}}{a}\right)]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Apply the power and quotient laws: [[\log_{a}\left(\frac{b^{2}}{a}\right)=2\log_{a}b-\log_{a}a]].

screens.title: You can reason with order and symbolic log information.
screens.body:
You have handled increasing and decreasing bases and extracted consequences from a given logarithmic relationship.

Next comes reciprocal substitution and linked logarithmic equations.

screens.title: Use a positive substitution for [[a^{x}+a^{-x}]]
screens.body:
Because [[a^{-x}=\frac{1}{a^{x}}]], an equation involving both terms can become a quadratic.

Let [[u=a^{x}]], where [[u>0]]. Then [[a^{-x}=\frac1u]]. Multiply through by u and solve the resulting quadratic.
screens.keyPoint: Reciprocal roots often correspond to opposite values of x.

screens.title: Solve a reciprocal exponential equation
screens.question: Solve [[2^{x}+2^{-x}=\frac52]].
screens.finalAnswer: The solutions are [[x=-1]] and [[x=1]], so the correct option is C.

screens.title: A sum and difference can reveal a product and quotient
screens.body:
If [[\log_{a}x+\log_{a}y=m]], then [[xy=a^{m}]].

If [[\log_{a}x-\log_{a}y=n]], then [[\frac{x}{y}=a^{n}]]. These two algebraic relationships can be solved together.
screens.keyPoint: The original logarithms automatically require [[x>0]] and [[y>0]].

screens.question: Positive numbers x and y satisfy [[\log_{2}x+\log_{2}y=3]] and [[\log_{2}x-\log_{2}y=1]]. What is [[x+y]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Adding the equations gives [[2\log_{2}x=4]], so [[x=4]]. Subtracting gives [[2\log_{2}y=2]], so [[y=2]]. Hence [[x+y=6]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Add the two equations to find x, then subtract them to find y. The logarithms have the same base, so this works like simultaneous linear equations.

screens.title: Advanced Log Reasoning: key points
keyPoints: Increasing exponential functions preserve inequalities; decreasing ones reverse them.
A fact such as [[\log_{a}b=k]] is equivalent to [[b=a^{k}]].
Use symbolic relationships and log laws before reaching for numerical approximations.
For [[a^{x}+a^{-x}]], substitute [[u=a^{x}>0]].
Sums and differences of logarithms can form simultaneous equations.
reviewItems: Check whether the exponential base is increasing or decreasing.
Translate a given logarithm into an exponential relationship.
Practise reciprocal substitutions.
Use positivity conditions to reject impossible values.
Next lesson: Exponentials and Logarithms Exam-style Questions.

screens.question: Evaluate [[\log_{2}32]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: A logarithm is an exponent. Ask which power of 2 equals 32.

screens.question: Which statement is equivalent to [[3^{4}=81]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[a^{b}=c]] is equivalent to [[\log_{a}c=b]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Keep the exponential base as the logarithm base, use the output as the argument, and use the exponent as the answer.

screens.question: For positive a with [[a\ne1]], simplify [[\log_{a}8+\log_{a}2]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The product law gives [[\log_{a}8+\log_{a}2=\log_{a}(8\times2)=\log_{a}16]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: When logarithms with the same base are added, multiply their arguments.

screens.question: Evaluate [[2\log_{3}6-\log_{3}4]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[2\log_{3}6-\log_{3}4=\log_{3}\left(\frac{6^{2}}4\right)=\log_{3}9=2]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the power law first, then the quotient law: [[2\log_{3}6=\log_{3}36]].

screens.question: For which values of x is [[\log(x-2)+\log(7-x)]] defined?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Both arguments must be positive: [[x-2>0]] and [[7-x>0]], so [[2<x<7]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: A logarithm's argument must be strictly positive. Intersect the conditions [[x>2]] and [[x<7]].

screens.question: Solve [[5^{2x-1}=125]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Since [[125=5^{3}]], equate exponents: [[2x-1=3]], giving [[x=2]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Rewrite 125 as a power of 5, then equate the exponents.

screens.question: Solve [[4^{x}=8^{x-1}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Write [[4=2^{2}]] and [[8=2^{3}]], simplify both powers, and equate the exponents.

screens.question: Solve [[9^{x}-10(3^{x})+9=0]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use [[u=3^{x}]], noting that [[9^{x}=u^{2}]]. Solve the quadratic and then substitute back.

screens.question: Solve [[\log_{2}(x-1)=3]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Convert to exponential form: the logarithm equation means [[x-1=2^{3}]].

screens.question: Solve [[\log_{3}x+\log_{3}(x-2)=1]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The domain is [[x>2]]. Combining logs gives [[x(x-2)=3]], so [[(x-3)(x+1)=0]]. Only [[x=3]] is valid.
screens.incorrectFeedback: First require [[x>2]]. Then use the product law and solve [[x(x-2)=3]], rejecting any value outside the domain.

screens.question: Solve [[\log_{5}(x+4)-\log_{5}x=1]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The quotient law gives [[\log_{5}\left(\frac{x+4}{x}\right)=1]], so [[\frac{x+4}{x}=5]] and [[x=1]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Combine the logarithms using the quotient law, then set the quotient equal to [[5^{1}]].

screens.question: Solve [[2\log_{2}x=\log_{2}49]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The power law gives [[\log_{2}(x^{2})=\log_{2}49]], so [[x^{2}=49]]. The original logarithm requires [[x>0]], leaving [[x=7]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: The algebra produces two square roots, but [[\log_{2}x]] is only defined when [[x>0]].

screens.question: Solve [[3^{2x-1}<\frac1{27}]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[\frac1{27}=3^{-3}]]. Since the base is greater than 1, [[2x-1<-3]], so [[x<-1]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Write the right side as [[3^{-3}]]. The function [[3^{x}]] is increasing, so the exponent inequality keeps its direction.

screens.question: Solve [[\left(\frac14\right)^{x}>16]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[16=\left(\frac14\right)^{-2}]]. The base lies between 0 and 1, so the function decreases and [[x<-2]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Express 16 as a power of one quarter. Because the base is decreasing, reverse the comparison between the exponents.

screens.question: Given [[\log_{a}b=2]], find [[\log_{a}\left(\frac{a^{5}}{b^{2}}\right)]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Apply the quotient and power laws: the expression becomes [[5-2\log_{a}b]].

screens.question: Positive numbers x and y satisfy [[\log_{2}x+\log_{2}y=5]] and [[\log_{2}x-\log_{2}y=1]]. Find [[x+y]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Adding gives [[2\log_{2}x=6]], so [[x=8]]. Subtracting gives [[2\log_{2}y=4]], so [[y=4]]. Therefore [[x+y=12]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Treat the two logarithms as unknowns. Add the equations to find x, then subtract them to find y.

screens.question: The real solutions of [[2^{x}+2^{-x}=\frac52]] are added together. What is the result?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Let [[u=2^{x}]]. Then [[2u^{2}-5u+2=0]], so [[u=2]] or [[u=\frac12]], giving [[x=1]] or [[x=-1]]. Their sum is 0.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use [[u=2^{x}]] and [[2^{-x}=\frac1u]]. The resulting quadratic has reciprocal roots.

screens.question: Which statement describes the graph of [[y=5^{x}]]?
options: It is increasing, passes through [[(0,1)]], and has asymptote [[y=0]].
It is decreasing, passes through [[(0,1)]], and has asymptote [[x=0]].
It is increasing, passes through [[(1,0)]], and crosses the x-axis.
It is decreasing and has range [[y<0]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Because [[5>1]], the graph increases. Every graph [[y=a^{x}]] passes through [[(0,1)]] and approaches, but never reaches, [[y=0]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: For a base greater than 1, the exponential graph increases. Its outputs are always positive and [[5^{0}=1]].

screens.question: For which values of k does [[2^{x}=k]] have exactly one real solution for x?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The function [[2^{x}]] is one-to-one and its range is [[k>0]], so each positive k gives exactly one real x.
screens.incorrectFeedback: The graph of [[y=2^{x}]] has range [[y>0]] and is strictly increasing.

screens.question: Solve [[2^{x}+4^{x}=6]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Let [[u=2^{x}>0]]. Then [[u+u^{2}=6]], so [[(u+3)(u-2)=0]]. Positivity leaves [[u=2]], hence [[x=1]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use [[u=2^{x}]], so [[4^{x}=u^{2}]]. Solve the quadratic and reject the negative value of u.

screens.title: Exponentials and Logarithms: exam review
keyPoints: A logarithm records an exponent: [[a^{b}=c]] is equivalent to [[\log_{a}c=b]].
Know the product, quotient, and power laws for logarithms.
Check logarithm domains before accepting algebraic solutions.
Match bases or use a positive substitution to solve exponential equations.
The direction of an exponential inequality depends on whether its base is above or below 1.
reviewItems: Redo any question where you forgot a domain restriction.
Practise moving quickly between logarithmic and exponential form.
Check that substituted exponential values are positive.
Sketch exponential graphs using their intercept, direction, range, and asymptote.
Repeat this set until each method is recognisable without prompting.

screens.title: A derivative measures instantaneous change

diagram.caption: At P, the derivative is the gradient of the tangent to the curve.

screens.body:
For [[y=f(x)]], the derivative [[f'(x)]] or [[\frac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x}]] gives the gradient of the tangent at each x-value.

A derivative can also represent a rate of change: velocity is the derivative of displacement with respect to time.
screens.keyPoint: Differentiation from first principles is not required for TMUA. You need to interpret derivatives and use the power rule accurately.

screens.title: Differentiate rational powers

steps.title: Use the rule
steps.body: For rational n, [[\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}x}(x^{n})=nx^{n-1}]]. Multiply by the old power, then reduce the power by 1.

screens.question: Given [[f(x)=\frac{3x+2}{\sqrt{x}}]], find [[f'(x)]].
screens.prompt: Rewrite the quotient using powers before differentiating.
screens.incorrectFeedback: First write [[f(x)=3x^{1/2}+2x^{-1/2}]]. Apply the power rule separately to both terms.

screens.title: The derivative supplies the gradient
screens.body:
At [[x=a]], the tangent gradient is [[f'(a)]]. The point on the curve is [[(a,f(a))]].

A normal is perpendicular to the tangent. If the tangent gradient is non-zero m, the normal gradient is [[-\frac1m]]. If the tangent is horizontal, the normal is the vertical line [[x=a]]. Use [[y-y_{1}=m(x-x_{1})]] for any non-vertical line.
screens.keyPoint: Always calculate both the point and the gradient. A correct gradient alone is not the equation of a tangent.

screens.title: Find an equation of a tangent
screens.question: Find the tangent to [[y=x^{2}+1]] at [[x=2]].

diagram.caption: The tangent touches the curve at the specified point and has gradient f′(2).

steps.title: Find the point
steps.body: At [[x=2]], [[y=2^{2}+1=5]], so the tangent passes through [[(2,5)]].

steps.title: Find the gradient
steps.body: [[\frac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x}=2x]], so the tangent gradient at [[x=2]] is 4.

screens.finalAnswer: The tangent is [[y=4x-3]], so the correct option is B.

screens.question: Which is the normal to [[y=x^{2}]] at [[x=1]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The tangent gradient is [[2x=2]], so the perpendicular normal gradient is [[-\frac12]]. The point is [[(1,1)]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Find the tangent gradient first, then take its negative reciprocal. The normal still passes through [[(1,1)]].

screens.title: You can differentiate powers and construct tangent lines.
screens.body:
You have connected derivatives with gradients and rates of change.

Next, derivatives will reveal turning points and the intervals on which a function rises or falls.

screens.title: At a stationary point, [[f'(x)=0]]

diagram.caption: At a local maximum or minimum, the tangent is horizontal.

screens.body:
A stationary point has a horizontal tangent, so solve [[f'(x)=0]] to find its x-coordinate. Substitute back into f to find its y-coordinate.

The second derivative, written [[f''(x)]] or [[\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2}y}{\mathrm{d}x^{2}}]], measures how the gradient changes. If it is negative at a stationary point, the point is a local maximum; if positive, it is a local minimum.
screens.keyPoint: The second derivative test classifies a candidate only after you have found it using the first derivative.

screens.title: Find the stationary points of [[f(x)=x^{3}-3x^{2}-9x+5]]

diagram.caption: The sign of the second derivative distinguishes the maximum from the minimum.

screens.question: What stationary point does [[f(x)=x^{2}-4x+7]] have?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[f'(x)=2x-4=0]] gives [[x=2]], and [[f(2)=3]]. Since [[f''(x)=2>0]], it is a local minimum.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Solve [[2x-4=0]], substitute the result into f, and use the sign of [[f''(x)=2]].

screens.title: The sign of [[f'(x)]] controls the direction
screens.body:
A function is strictly increasing wherever [[f'(x)>0]] and strictly decreasing wherever [[f'(x)<0]].

Find the zeros of the derivative, then use a sign chart or test values to determine its sign on each interval.
screens.keyPoint: Do not solve [[f(x)>0]]. The height of a function and the direction in which it is moving are different questions.

screens.title: Find intervals of increase and decrease
screens.question: For [[f(x)=x^{3}-3x]], where is f strictly increasing?

steps.title: Find the critical values
steps.body: [[f'(x)=0]] at [[x=-1]] and [[x=1]]. These values split the number line into three intervals.

steps.title: Check the sign
steps.body: The product [[3(x-1)(x+1)]] is positive for [[x<-1]], negative for [[-1<x<1]], and positive for [[x>1]].

steps.title: State the intervals
steps.body: Therefore f is strictly increasing for [[x<-1]] or [[x>1]].

screens.finalAnswer: The increasing intervals are [[x<-1]] and [[x>1]], so the correct option is D.

screens.question: A particle has displacement [[s(t)=t^{3}-6t^{2}+9t]]. What is its velocity when [[t=2]]?
screens.incorrectFeedback: Velocity is the derivative of displacement. Differentiate s with respect to t, then substitute [[t=2]].

screens.title: Differentiation and Turning Points: key points
keyPoints: [[f'(x)]] is the tangent gradient and an instantaneous rate of change.
Use [[\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}x}(x^{n})=nx^{n-1}]] for rational powers.
A normal gradient is the negative reciprocal of the non-zero tangent gradient.
Stationary points satisfy [[f'(x)=0]]; the second derivative can classify maxima and minima.
A function increases where [[f'(x)>0]] and decreases where [[f'(x)<0]].
reviewItems: Rewrite roots and reciprocal powers before differentiating.
Use both a point and a gradient when forming a tangent or normal.
Substitute stationary x-values back into the original function.
Build sign charts from the derivative, not from the original function.
Next lesson: Integration and Area.

screens.title: Integration reverses differentiation
screens.body:
For rational [[n\ne-1]], [[\int x^{n}\,\mathrm{d}x=\frac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}+C]]. Increase the power by 1, then divide by the new power.

The constant C is required because every constant disappears when differentiated.
screens.keyPoint: Differentiate your answer mentally to check it. An indefinite integral without [[+C]] is incomplete.

screens.title: Integrate powers term by term

screens.question: Find [[\int\left(3\sqrt{x}+\frac{2}{x^{2}}\right)\,\mathrm{d}x]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Rewrite as [[3x^{1/2}+2x^{-2}]]. Integrating gives [[2x^{3/2}-2x^{-1}+C]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Rewrite the root and reciprocal as powers. Increase each power by 1 and divide by the new power.

screens.title: Use an antiderivative at the limits
screens.body:
If [[F'(x)=f(x)]], the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus gives [[\int_{a}^{b}f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x=F(b)-F(a)]].

A definite integral is a number, so there is no [[+C]]. Use square-bracket notation to avoid substituting into the wrong expression.
screens.keyPoint: Upper limit minus lower limit: [[\left[F(x)\right]_{a}^{b}=F(b)-F(a)]].

screens.title: Evaluate a definite integral
screens.question: Find [[\int_{1}^{3}(2x+1)\,\mathrm{d}x]].

steps.title: Subtract the lower value
steps.body: At [[x=1]], the value is 2. Therefore the integral is [[12-2=10]].

screens.finalAnswer: The value is 10, so the correct option is B.

screens.question: Evaluate [[\int_{0}^{2}(3x^{2}-1)\,\mathrm{d}x]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: An antiderivative is [[x^{3}-x]]. Evaluate it at 2 and subtract its value at 0.

screens.title: You can integrate powers and evaluate between limits.
screens.body:
You have used integration as the reverse of differentiation and applied the Fundamental Theorem.

Next, we will separate signed integrals from geometric area.

screens.title: A definite integral can be negative

diagram.caption: Area above the x-axis contributes positively; area below contributes negatively.

screens.body:
The integral [[\int_{a}^{b}f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x]] is signed area. Regions above the x-axis count positively and regions below count negatively.

Geometric area is always non-negative. If a curve crosses the axis, split the interval at each root and make every region positive.
screens.keyPoint: Integral and area are equal only when the curve does not go below the x-axis on the interval.

screens.title: Find the area between [[y=x^{2}-1]] and the x-axis for [[0\le x\le2]]

diagram.caption: Split an area question wherever the curve crosses the x-axis.

steps.title: Locate the crossing
steps.body: [[x^{2}-1=0]] gives [[x=1]] in the interval. The function is negative from 0 to 1 and positive from 1 to 2.

steps.title: Add positive regions
steps.body: The total geometric area is [[\frac23+\frac43=2]]. The unsplit signed integral would instead be [[\frac23]].

screens.question: You are told only that [[\int_{0}^{4}f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x=-3]]. What is the area between the graph and the x-axis?
options: At least 3, but its exact value cannot be determined
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The signed total is -3, but positive and negative regions may cancel. The geometric area is at least 3, and more information is needed to find it exactly.
screens.incorrectFeedback: A definite integral is signed. Without knowing where f is positive or negative, you cannot reconstruct the total geometric area.

screens.title: Join contiguous intervals and reverse with a sign change
screens.body:
For adjacent intervals, [[\int_{a}^{b}f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x+\int_{b}^{c}f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x=\int_{a}^{c}f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x]].

Reversing the limits changes the sign: [[\int_{b}^{a}f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x=-\int_{a}^{b}f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x]]. Integrals over the same interval can also be added term by term.
screens.keyPoint: Treat limits like directed journeys: going from b back to a reverses the sign.

screens.question: Given [[\int_{1}^{4}f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x=7]] and [[\int_{4}^{6}f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x=-2]], find [[\int_{1}^{6}f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The intervals are contiguous, so [[\int_{1}^{6}f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x=7+(-2)=5]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Join the journey from 1 to 4 with the journey from 4 to 6. Add the two signed values.

screens.title: Differentiating an integral recovers the integrand
screens.body:
The Fundamental Theorem also gives [[\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}x}\left(\int_{a}^{x}f(t)\,\mathrm{d}t\right)=f(x)]].

The integral accumulates signed area as its upper limit moves. Its instantaneous rate of change is therefore the current height of the original function.
screens.keyPoint: The integration variable t is a placeholder. After differentiating with respect to x, replace it with x.

screens.title: Integration and Area: key points
keyPoints: For [[n\ne-1]], [[\int x^{n}\,\mathrm{d}x=\frac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}+C]].
Definite integrals use [[F(b)-F(a)]] and do not need an arbitrary constant.
A definite integral is signed area; geometric area is always non-negative.
Split an area calculation wherever the curve crosses the x-axis.
Contiguous integrals join, and reversing the limits reverses the sign.
reviewItems: Rewrite roots and reciprocals as powers before integrating.
Differentiate indefinite answers to check them.
Keep upper-minus-lower substitution in clear brackets.
Distinguish signed integral from total geometric area.
Next lesson: Trapezium Rule and Integration Logic.

screens.title: Approximate a curved region with straight-sided strips

diagram.caption: At equal spacing h, neighbouring ordinates form a trapezium.

screens.body:
For n equal strips of width h with ordinates [[y_{0},y_{1},\ldots,y_{n}]], the trapezium rule is

[[\frac{h}{2}\left(y_{0}+2(y_{1}+\cdots+y_{n-1})+y_{n}\right)]].
screens.keyPoint: The first and last ordinates appear once. Every interior ordinate appears twice.

screens.title: Estimate an integral from four ordinates

diagram.caption: The number of strips is one less than the number of ordinates.

steps.title: Read the table
steps.body: Suppose x-values [[0,1,2,3]] have y-values [[1,2,5,10]]. There are 3 strips and [[h=1]].

steps.title: Identify the endpoints
steps.body: The first and last values are 1 and 10. The interior values are 2 and 5.

screens.question: At [[x=0,2,4]], a function has values [[3,5,9]]. Use the trapezium rule to estimate [[\int_{0}^{4}f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: There are two strips of width 2. Count the middle ordinate twice and the two endpoints once.

screens.title: The direction of curvature determines the error

diagram.caption: Each trapezium uses a chord instead of the curved section of the graph.

screens.body:
The trapezium rule replaces the curve by straight chords. If the graph is concave upwards, the chords lie above it and the estimate is too large.

If the graph is concave downwards, the chords lie below it and the estimate is too small.
screens.keyPoint: Where [[f''(x)>0]], expect an overestimate. Where [[f''(x)<0]], expect an underestimate.

screens.question: The trapezium rule is used for [[y=x^{2}]] on [[0\le x\le2]]. Which statement is correct?
options: It is an underestimate because the graph is increasing.
It is an overestimate because the graph is concave upwards.
It is exact because the function is a polynomial.
The direction of the error cannot be determined.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Since [[f''(x)=2>0]], the graph is concave upwards and its chords lie above the curve.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Increasing or decreasing is not the deciding factor. For [[x^{2}]], the positive second derivative means the graph bends upwards.

screens.title: Compare an estimate with the exact integral
screens.question: Use two equal strips for [[\int_{0}^{2}x^{2}\,\mathrm{d}x]]. By how much does the trapezium estimate exceed the exact value?

diagram.caption: For a concave-up curve, the straight chords enclose extra area.

steps.title: Find the ordinates
steps.body: With two strips, [[h=1]]. At [[x=0,1,2]], the values of [[x^{2}]] are [[0,1,4]].

screens.finalAnswer: The estimate exceeds the exact value by [[\frac13]], so the correct option is B.

screens.title: You can calculate and interpret trapezium estimates.
screens.body:
You have used tables, counted ordinates correctly, and determined whether an estimate is above or below the true value.

Next, integration will recover a function from its derivative.

screens.title: Recover y from [[\frac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x}=f(x)]]
screens.body:
For the differential equations required by TMUA, the derivative is a function of x alone. Integrate both sides to obtain

[[y=\int f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x=F(x)+C]].

A given point or initial value determines C.
screens.keyPoint: The integration constant represents a whole family of curves. An initial condition selects one member of that family.

screens.title: Use an initial condition
screens.question: Given [[\frac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x}=6x-4]] and [[y=3]] when [[x=1]], find y in terms of x.
screens.finalAnswer: The particular solution is [[y=3x^{2}-4x+4]], so the correct option is B.

screens.question: Given [[\frac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x}=3x^{2}]] and [[y=5]] when [[x=0]], find y.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Integrating gives [[y=x^{3}+C]]. The condition [[y(0)=5]] gives [[C=5]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Integrate [[3x^{2}]] first, include C, and then use the value at [[x=0]].

screens.title: Integrating a rate gives the net change
screens.body:
If [[F'(x)=f(x)]], then [[F(b)-F(a)=\int_{a}^{b}f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x]].

This connects rate-of-change models with definite integration. Integrate the rate over an interval to find the change, then add the initial value to find the final value.
screens.keyPoint: Final amount = initial amount + accumulated change.

screens.title: A quantity V changes at rate [[\frac{\mathrm{d}V}{\mathrm{d}t}=4t+1]]

screens.question: Given [[F'(x)=f(x)]], [[\int_{2}^{5}f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x=9]], and [[F(2)=4]], find [[F(5)]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: The integral gives the change in F from 2 to 5. Add that change to the initial value [[F(2)=4]].

screens.title: Trapezium Rule and Integration Logic: key points
keyPoints: With equal spacing h, use [[\frac{h}{2}(y_{0}+2(y_{1}+\cdots+y_{n-1})+y_{n})]].
There is one fewer strip than there are ordinates.
Concave-up graphs give trapezium overestimates; concave-down graphs give underestimates.
Integrate [[\frac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x}=f(x)]] and use an initial condition to determine C.
The integral of a rate over an interval gives the net change.
reviewItems: Check h from the x-values rather than assuming it is 1.
Count endpoints once and interior ordinates twice.
Use curvature, not whether the function rises, to judge the error.
Keep the arbitrary constant until an initial condition determines it.
Next lesson: Calculus Exam-style Questions.

screens.question: Differentiate [[4x^{3}-3\sqrt{x}]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Rewrite [[\sqrt{x}=x^{1/2}]], then apply the power rule to get [[12x^{2}-\frac32x^{-1/2}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Rewrite the root as [[x^{1/2}]]. Multiply by each old power, then subtract 1 from that power.

screens.question: If [[f(x)=\frac{x^{2}+1}{x}]], which expression equals [[f'(x)]]?
screens.incorrectFeedback: Simplify before differentiating: divide both terms in the numerator by x.

screens.question: Find the tangent to [[y=x^{3}]] at [[x=1]].

diagram.caption: Use the derivative for the gradient and the original function for the point.

screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The point is [[(1,1)]] and the gradient is [[3x^{2}=3]]. Thus [[y-1=3(x-1)]], or [[y=3x-2]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Calculate both the point and [[f'(1)]], then use point-gradient form.

screens.question: Which is the normal to [[y=\sqrt{x}]] at [[x=4]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[\frac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x}=\frac1{2\sqrt{x}}]], so the tangent gradient at 4 is [[\frac14]]. The normal gradient is -4.
screens.incorrectFeedback: The tangent gradient is [[\frac1{2\sqrt4}=\frac14]]. Take its negative reciprocal and use the point [[(4,2)]].

screens.question: What stationary point does [[f(x)=x^{2}+6x+10]] have?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[f'(x)=2x+6=0]] gives [[x=-3]], and [[f(-3)=1]]. Since [[f''(x)=2>0]], it is a minimum.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Solve [[f'(x)=0]], substitute into f, then use the positive second derivative.

screens.question: Where is [[f(x)=x^{3}-12x]] strictly increasing?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[f'(x)=3x^{2}-12=3(x-2)(x+2)]], which is positive outside the roots -2 and 2.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Increasing means [[f'(x)>0]]. Factor the derivative and make a sign chart around -2 and 2.

screens.question: A particle has displacement [[s(t)=2t^{3}-5t]]. Find its velocity at [[t=2]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Velocity is the derivative of displacement. Differentiate first and then substitute 2.

screens.question: The function [[f(x)=x^{4}-2x^{2}]] has a stationary point at [[x=0]]. How is it classified?

diagram.caption: Use the second derivative at the stationary x-value.

screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[f''(x)=12x^{2}-4]], so [[f''(0)=-4<0]]. The stationary point is a local maximum.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Differentiate twice. A negative second derivative at a stationary point indicates a local maximum.

screens.question: Find [[\int(4x^{3}-6x^{-2})\,\mathrm{d}x]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[\int4x^{3}\,\mathrm{d}x=x^{4}]] and [[\int-6x^{-2}\,\mathrm{d}x=6x^{-1}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Increase each power by 1 and divide by the new power. Be careful: dividing -6 by -1 changes the sign.

screens.question: Evaluate [[\int_{1}^{2}3x^{2}\,\mathrm{d}x]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: An antiderivative of [[3x^{2}]] is [[x^{3}]]. Evaluate upper minus lower.

screens.question: Evaluate the signed integral [[\int_{0}^{3}(2x-3)\,\mathrm{d}x]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[\left[x^{2}-3x\right]_{0}^{3}=0]]. Equal negative and positive regions cancel.
