screens.incorrectFeedback: Use an antiderivative, not total geometric area. The region below the axis contributes negatively.

screens.question: Find the total area between [[y=x-1]] and the x-axis for [[0\le x\le3]].

diagram.caption: For total area, make both the below-axis and above-axis regions positive.

screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The negative triangle from 0 to 1 has area [[\frac12]], and the positive triangle from 1 to 3 has area 2. The total is [[\frac52]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Split at the root [[x=1]]. Calculate both triangular regions as positive areas before adding.

screens.question: Given [[\int_{0}^{2}f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x=3]] and [[\int_{2}^{5}f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x=-7]], find [[\int_{5}^{0}f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. First [[\int_{0}^{5}f=3-7=-4]]. Reversing the limits changes the sign, so [[\int_{5}^{0}f=4]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Join the contiguous intervals first, then reverse the limits with a sign change.

screens.question: Find [[\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}x}\left(\int_{2}^{x}(t^{2}+1)\,\mathrm{d}t\right)]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. By the Fundamental Theorem, differentiating an accumulation integral returns the integrand at its upper limit: [[x^{2}+1]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Replace the integration variable t by the moving upper limit x after applying the Fundamental Theorem.

screens.question: At [[x=0,1,2,3]], the ordinates of a curve are [[2,3,6,11]]. Use the trapezium rule to estimate the integral from 0 to 3.

diagram.caption: There are three strips of width 1.

screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the endpoints once and the two interior ordinates twice. Here [[h=1]].

screens.question: The trapezium rule is applied to [[f(x)=-x^{2}+6]] on an interval. What type of estimate does it give?
options: An overestimate because f is positive
An exact value because f is quadratic
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[f''(x)=-2<0]], so the graph is concave down and its chords lie below the curve.
screens.incorrectFeedback: The direction is controlled by curvature. A negative second derivative produces a trapezium underestimate.

screens.question: Given [[\frac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x}=4x^{3}]] and [[y=3]] when [[x=1]], find y.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Integrating gives [[y=x^{4}+C]]. The condition [[3=1+C]] gives [[C=2]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Integrate the derivative and retain C. Then substitute the given point to determine it.

screens.question: A quantity Q satisfies [[\frac{\mathrm{d}Q}{\mathrm{d}t}=6t-2]] and [[Q(0)=5]]. Find [[Q(2)]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The change is [[\int_{0}^{2}(6t-2)\,\mathrm{d}t=\left[3t^{2}-2t\right]_{0}^{2}=8]]. Add the initial 5 to get 13.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Integrate the rate from 0 to 2 to find the change, then add the initial value.

screens.question: Given [[\int_{1}^{4}f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x=6]] and [[\int_{1}^{4}g(x)\,\mathrm{d}x=-2]], evaluate [[\int_{1}^{4}(2f(x)-g(x))\,\mathrm{d}x]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. By linearity, the value is [[2(6)-(-2)=14]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Integrate term by term: double the integral of f, then subtract the integral of g.

screens.question: A function F is continuous on [[a\le x\le b]] and differentiable on [[a\lt x\lt b]], with [[F'(x)\gt0]] throughout [[a\lt x\lt b]]. Which conclusion must be true?
options: F has a stationary point between a and b
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. A positive derivative throughout the interval means F is strictly increasing, so [[F(a)<F(b)]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: The sign of the derivative controls direction. A strictly positive derivative means the function rises as x increases.

screens.title: Calculus: exam review
keyPoints: Simplify algebraic expressions before applying the differentiation or integration power rule.
Derivatives control gradients, rates, stationary points, and intervals of increase or decrease.
Definite integrals give signed accumulation; geometric area may require splitting at roots.
Use the Fundamental Theorem to connect derivatives, integrals, and total change.
Apply the trapezium rule with correct spacing and use curvature to judge its error.
reviewItems: Redo questions where a root, reciprocal, or fractional power caused an error.
Check tangent and normal equations with the stated point.
Keep signed integral and total area conceptually separate.
Practise trapezium calculations without assuming the spacing is 1.
Repeat this set until every method is recognisable from the question structure.

screens.title: Recognise the parent graph before transforming it
screens.body:
You should recognise the main shapes of lines, quadratics, cubics, reciprocal, square-root, exponential, logarithmic, trigonometric, and modulus functions.

Start from intercepts, symmetry, turning points, asymptotes, domain, and range. These features move predictably under transformations.
screens.keyPoint: A sketch needs the right structure, not perfect scale. Mark decisive features such as roots, vertices, and asymptotes.

screens.title: Changes outside f are vertical; changes inside are horizontal

diagram.caption: Track distinctive points on the parent graph to construct the transformed graph.

screens.body:
[[y=f(x)+a]] moves the graph a units vertically. Positive a moves it up.

[[y=f(x+a)]] moves the graph a units horizontally in the opposite-looking direction: positive a moves it left.
screens.keyPoint: Inside changes act on x before f is evaluated, which is why their horizontal direction appears reversed.

screens.title: Transform a known point [[P=(x,y)]]

steps.title: Vertical scaling
steps.body: On [[Y=af(x)]], the point becomes [[(x,ay)]]. x stays fixed.

steps.title: Horizontal scaling
steps.body: On [[Y=f(ax)]], the point becomes [[\left(\frac{x}{a},y\right)]]. Horizontal coordinates are divided by a.

screens.question: The point [[(2,5)]] lies on [[y=f(x)]]. Which point lies on [[y=f(x-3)+2]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Replacing x by [[x-3]] moves the graph right by 3, and adding 2 moves it up by 2. Thus [[(2,5)\mapsto(5,7)]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: The inside change moves the point right by 3. The outside change then raises its y-coordinate by 2.

screens.title: A negative multiplier introduces a reflection
screens.body:
[[y=-f(x)]] reflects the graph in the x-axis, while [[y=f(-x)]] reflects it in the y-axis.

For [[y=af(x)]], [[\lvert a\rvert>1]] gives a vertical stretch and [[0<\lvert a\rvert<1]] a vertical compression. For [[y=f(ax)]], the horizontal scale factor is [[\frac1{\lvert a\rvert}]].
screens.keyPoint: Outside multipliers change y-coordinates. Inside multipliers change x-coordinates reciprocally.

screens.title: Compose several transformations
screens.question: The point [[(6,-1)]] lies on [[y=f(x)]]. Where does it move on [[y=-2f(3x)+1]]?

diagram.caption: Apply the horizontal mapping to x and the outside mapping to y.

steps.title: Handle the vertical multiplier
steps.body: The y-coordinate is multiplied by -2, so [[-1\mapsto2]]. This also reflects the graph in the x-axis.

screens.finalAnswer: The image is [[(2,3)]], so the correct option is B.

screens.title: You can now move points instead of guessing whole sketches.
screens.body:
You have handled translations, stretches, compressions, reflections, and compositions.

Next, we connect function composition with graph parameters and intersections.

screens.title: In [[f(g(x))]], apply g first
screens.body:
The output of g becomes the input of f. Work from the inside out.

For example, if [[f(x)=x^{2}+1]] and [[g(x)=2x-3]], then [[f(g(x))=(2x-3)^{2}+1]], whereas [[g(f(x))=2(x^{2}+1)-3]]. They are generally different.
screens.keyPoint: Function composition is ordered: [[f\circ g]] need not equal [[g\circ f]].

screens.question: Let [[f(x)=x^{2}+1]] and [[g(x)=2x-3]]. Find [[f(g(2))]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Evaluate the inner function first: find [[g(2)]], then use that result as the input to f.

screens.title: Read key features directly from standard forms
screens.body:
For [[y=mx+c]], m controls the gradient and c the y-intercept.

For [[y=a(x+b)^{2}+c]], the vertex is [[(-b,c)]]. The sign of a decides whether the parabola opens up or down, while [[\lvert a\rvert]] controls how narrow it is.
screens.keyPoint: The vertex x-coordinate has the opposite sign to the number inside the bracket.

screens.question: Which statement describes [[y=-2(x+3)^{2}+5]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The vertex is [[(-3,5)]], and the negative coefficient means the parabola opens down, so this is a maximum.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Read the vertex from [[a(x+b)^{2}+c]] as [[(-b,c)]], then use the sign of a.

screens.title: Algebraic solutions are geometric intersections

diagram.caption: Each intersection gives an x-value satisfying f(x) = g(x).

screens.body:
Set [[x=0]] to find a y-intercept and set [[y=0]] to find roots or x-intercepts.

The solutions of [[f(x)=g(x)]] are the x-coordinates where the two graphs meet. A polynomial of degree n can have at most n real roots, though it may have fewer.
screens.keyPoint: Differentiation can add shape information: stationary points and derivative signs help determine how many intersections are possible.

screens.question: At which x-values do [[y=x^{2}]] and [[y=2x+3]] intersect?
screens.incorrectFeedback: Equate the two y-expressions, rearrange to a quadratic, and factorise.

screens.title: Graph Transformations: key points
keyPoints: Recognise parent graphs from their intercepts, symmetry, turning points, domains, ranges, and asymptotes.
Outside changes act vertically; inside changes act horizontally and reciprocally.
[[f(-x)]] reflects in the y-axis, while [[-f(x)]] reflects in the x-axis.
In [[f(g(x))]], evaluate g before f.
Roots and simultaneous solutions are intersections with axes or other graphs.
reviewItems: Track known points through transformations.
Remember that [[f(x+a)]] moves left when a is positive.
Read quadratic vertices directly from completed-square form.
Use algebra and differentiation together when reasoning about graph shape.
Next lesson: Geometry and Proportion.

screens.title: Turn proportional language into an equation
screens.body:
If y is directly proportional to [[x^{n}]], write [[y=kx^{n}]]. If y is inversely proportional to [[x^{n}]], write [[y=\frac{k}{x^{n}}]].

Use one pair of values to determine k, then keep the same k throughout the problem. Repeated percentage growth is also multiplicative: an increase of [[p\%]] for n stages multiplies an initial value V by [[\left(1+\frac{p}{100}\right)^{n}]].
screens.keyPoint: Proportion and growth are multiplicative. A statement such as [[y\propto x^{2}]] does not mean [[y=x^{2}+c]].

screens.title: Use the constant of proportionality

screens.question: The quantity y is inversely proportional to [[\sqrt{x}]]. If x is multiplied by 9, what happens to y?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[y=\frac{k}{\sqrt{x}}]]. Multiplying x by 9 multiplies [[\sqrt{x}]] by 3, so y is divided by 3.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Write [[y=kx^{-1/2}]]. A factor of 9 in x becomes a factor of [[9^{-1/2}=\frac13]] in y.

screens.title: Length, area, and volume scale by different powers

diagram.caption: A length scale factor of 2 produces an area factor of 4.

screens.body:
For similar figures with length scale factor k, corresponding lengths scale by k, areas by [[k^{2}]], and volumes by [[k^{3}]].

All corresponding angles are equal. Congruent figures are the special case [[k=1]].
screens.keyPoint: Identify whether the quantity is one-, two-, or three-dimensional before applying the scale factor.

screens.title: Scale the volume of a similar solid
screens.question: Two similar solids have corresponding lengths in the ratio [[2:3]]. The smaller has volume 64. Find the larger volume.

diagram.caption: The linear scale factor is one and a half, so the volume factor is its cube.

steps.title: Check the direction
steps.body: The larger solid must have the larger volume, so 216 is sensible.

screens.finalAnswer: The larger volume is 216, so the correct option is D.

screens.question: Two similar shapes have corresponding lengths in the ratio [[1:3]]. What is the ratio of their areas?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Area uses the square of the length factor, so [[1^{2}:3^{2}=1:9]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Square both parts of the length ratio. Cubing would apply to volume, not area.

screens.title: You can convert proportional relationships and scale similar figures.
screens.body:
You have used constants of proportionality and separated length, area, and volume factors.

Next, we bring together angle facts, transformations, measurement, circles, and vectors.

screens.title: Build unfamiliar geometry from standard facts
screens.body:
Angles on a line total 180°, angles around a point total 360°, and vertically opposite angles are equal. Parallel lines create equal corresponding and alternate angles.

An n-sided polygon has interior-angle sum [[180(n-2)]] degrees and exterior-angle sum 360°. A regular polygon therefore has exterior angle [[\frac{360}{n}]] degrees.
screens.keyPoint: Mark equal and supplementary angles directly on the diagram before forming equations.

screens.question: A regular polygon has exterior angle 24°. How many sides does it have?
screens.incorrectFeedback: The exterior angles of any polygon total 360°. Divide 360 by the regular exterior angle.

screens.title: Know what each transformation preserves
screens.body:
Triangles are congruent by SSS, SAS, ASA, or RHS. Rotation, reflection, and translation preserve lengths and angles; enlargement preserves angles but scales lengths.

A negative enlargement scale factor places the image on the opposite side of the centre. A translation is described by a two-dimensional vector.
screens.keyPoint: SSA is not a general congruence criterion. Two sides and a non-included angle may produce different triangles.

screens.question: An enlargement with centre the origin and scale factor -2 maps [[(3,-1)]] to which point?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Multiply both coordinates by -2: [[(3,-1)\mapsto(-6,2)]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: For an enlargement centred at the origin, multiply the entire position vector by the scale factor.

screens.title: Reduce three-dimensional problems to right triangles
screens.body:
Use [[a^{2}+b^{2}=c^{2}]] in two or three dimensions, one right triangle at a time. A prism has volume equal to cross-sectional area times length.

Also recall [[C=2\pi r]], [[A=\pi r^{2}]], and, in radians, arc length [[r\theta]] and sector area [[\frac12r^{2}\theta]]. Bearings are measured clockwise from north and written with three figures.
screens.keyPoint: Plans and elevations are two-dimensional views. Match visible lengths carefully before applying a formula.

screens.title: Find a cuboid's space diagonal
screens.question: A cuboid has side lengths 3, 4, and 12. Find the distance between opposite vertices.

steps.title: Form a second right triangle
steps.body: The base diagonal 5 and vertical side 12 are perpendicular.

screens.finalAnswer: The space diagonal is 13, so the correct option is B.

screens.title: Use radii, chords, tangents, and subtended angles

diagram.caption: A radius is perpendicular to the tangent at the point of contact.

screens.body:
The angle at the centre is twice the angle at the circumference on the same arc. An angle in a semicircle is 90°, angles in the same segment are equal, and opposite angles in a cyclic quadrilateral sum to 180°.

The angle between a tangent and a chord equals the angle in the alternate segment.
screens.keyPoint: Join a tangent point to the centre: the resulting radius creates a right angle.

screens.question: A chord subtends an angle of 38° at the circumference. What angle does the same chord subtend at the centre?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The angle at the centre is twice the angle at the circumference, so [[2\times38^{\circ}=76^{\circ}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: For the same arc, double the angle at the circumference to obtain the angle at the centre.

screens.title: Express routes as vector sums
screens.body:
If position vectors of A and B are a and b, then [[\overrightarrow{AB}=\mathbf{b}-\mathbf{a}]]. The midpoint has position vector [[\frac12(\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b})]].

Parallel vectors are scalar multiples. This lets you prove collinearity, parallelism, and fixed ratios without measuring a diagram.
screens.keyPoint: A valid vector proof must explain both direction and scalar relationship, not merely quote matching-looking expressions.

screens.question: Points A and B have position vectors a and b. What is the position vector of the midpoint of AB?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The midpoint averages the two position vectors: [[\frac12(\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b})]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: A midpoint is the coordinate-wise average of its endpoints, so average their position vectors.

screens.title: Geometry and Proportion: key points
keyPoints: Translate direct and inverse proportion into equations containing a constant k.
A length factor k produces area factor [[k^{2}]] and volume factor [[k^{3}]].
Combine angle, congruence, similarity, and transformation facts systematically.
Use Pythagoras in stages for three-dimensional distances.
Circle theorems and vector relationships turn diagrams into exact equations.
reviewItems: Decide whether a proportional model uses x, [[x^{2}]], or a reciprocal power.
Separate length, area, and volume scale factors.
Do not use SSA as a general congruence test.
Add helpful radii and chords to circle diagrams.
Next lesson: Statistics and Probability.

screens.title: Match the diagram to the type of data
screens.body:
Bar charts and pie charts suit categorical data; vertical line charts suit ungrouped discrete data; line graphs show time series.

Histograms represent grouped numerical data using area, and cumulative-frequency graphs reveal medians, quartiles, and percentiles.
screens.keyPoint: Histogram bars touch because the underlying scale is continuous. Ordinary bar-chart gaps separate categories.

screens.question: Which diagram is most appropriate for displaying the distribution of 500 measured journey times grouped into unequal class intervals?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Journey time is continuous, grouped numerical data. Unequal intervals require frequency density so bar area represents frequency.
screens.incorrectFeedback: The data are continuous and grouped into unequal widths, which is precisely the setting for a frequency-density histogram.

screens.title: Compare centre and spread like for like
screens.body:
For ungrouped data, calculate exact mean, median, mode, and range. For grouped data, use class midpoints to estimate the mean and interpolate or read graphs to estimate quartiles.

Median and interquartile range are a natural pair: median measures typical position and IQR measures the spread of the middle half.
screens.keyPoint: Grouped statistics are estimates because the exact values inside each class are unknown.

screens.title: Estimate a grouped mean
screens.question: Values in intervals [[0\le x<10]], [[10\le x<20]], and [[20\le x<30]] have frequencies 2, 5, and 3. Estimate the mean.
screens.finalAnswer: The estimated mean is 16, so the correct option is C.

screens.question: Set A has median 50 and IQR 8. Set B has median 55 and IQR 14. Which comparison is justified?
options: A has the higher typical value and greater spread.
B has the higher typical value, while A is more consistent.
B is more consistent because its median is larger.
The sets cannot be compared without their means.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. B's larger median indicates a higher typical value. A's smaller IQR indicates less spread in the middle half.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Compare medians with medians and IQRs with IQRs. A smaller IQR means greater consistency.

screens.title: You can choose representations and compare distributions.
screens.body:
You have distinguished data types, estimated grouped means, and paired measures of centre and spread.

Next, we interpret histograms, cumulative frequency, and scatter data.

screens.title: Histogram area represents frequency

diagram.caption: Unequal class widths require frequency density on the vertical axis.

screens.body:
Use [[\text{frequency density}=\frac{\text{frequency}}{\text{class width}}]]. Therefore [[\text{frequency}=\text{class width}\times\text{frequency density}]].

A taller bar need not contain more observations if it is much narrower.
screens.keyPoint: Compare bar areas for frequency, not heights alone.

screens.question: A histogram class has width 5 and frequency density 3. What is its frequency?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Frequency is bar area: [[5\times3=15]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Multiply class width by frequency density.

screens.title: Use positions to estimate quartiles

diagram.caption: Read horizontally from a cumulative position to the curve, then down to the data scale.

screens.body:
For N observations, estimate [[Q_{1}]] at cumulative frequency [[\frac{N}{4}]], the median at [[\frac{N}{2}]], and [[Q_{3}]] at [[\frac{3N}{4}]].

The interquartile range is [[Q_{3}-Q_{1}]].
screens.keyPoint: The vertical axis shows how many observations are at or below a value.

screens.question: A cumulative-frequency graph represents 80 observations. At which cumulative frequencies are [[Q_{1}]], the median, and [[Q_{3}]] read?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The positions are [[\frac{80}{4}=20]], [[\frac{80}{2}=40]], and [[\frac{3(80)}4=60]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use one quarter, one half, and three quarters of the total frequency.

screens.title: Correlation describes association, not cause

diagram.caption: A line of best fit summarises an apparent trend; it does not prove a causal mechanism.

screens.body:
Scatter graphs can show positive, negative, or no correlation. A line of best fit supports interpolation within the observed range.

Extrapolation beyond the data is less reliable, and even strong correlation does not establish that one variable causes the other.
screens.keyPoint: A third variable or selection effect may explain an association.

screens.question: A strong positive correlation is observed between two variables X and Y. Which conclusion is always valid?
options: Increasing X causes Y to increase.
Increasing Y causes X to increase.
The observed values tend to increase together.
Every point lies exactly on a straight line.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Correlation describes an observed tendency. It does not by itself determine causation or require a perfect line.
screens.incorrectFeedback: A scatter graph supports a statement about association, not a causal direction.

screens.title: Know when to add and when to multiply
screens.body:
Probabilities lie from 0 to 1, and exhaustive outcomes total 1. Add probabilities for mutually exclusive alternatives.

Multiply along successive branches: [[P(A\text{ and }B)=P(A)P(B\mid A)]]. For independent events, [[P(B\mid A)=P(B)]]. Expected frequency is probability times number of trials.
screens.keyPoint: Without replacement, later probabilities usually change because the contents of the sample space have changed.

screens.title: Multiply along a complete route

diagram.caption: A route through the tree represents an intersection of events.

steps.title: Read the conditional branch
steps.body: Suppose [[P(B\mid A)=0.7]]. This is the probability of B after A has occurred.

steps.title: Add only complete alternatives
steps.body: To find B by any route, add the probabilities of all mutually exclusive routes ending in B.

screens.question: A bag contains 3 red and 2 blue counters. Two are drawn without replacement. What is the probability that both are red?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[\frac35\times\frac24=\frac3{10}]]. The second numerator and denominator both change after a red counter is removed.
screens.incorrectFeedback: After the first red counter, 2 red counters remain among 4 total counters.

screens.title: Restrict the denominator to the given condition
screens.body:
In [[P(A\mid B)]], the event B defines the new sample space. Use only outcomes inside B in the denominator.

Two-way tables, Venn diagrams, and expected-frequency trees make this restriction visible without requiring formal set notation.
screens.keyPoint: The denominator answers: out of how many cases satisfying the condition?

screens.title: Read a conditional probability from counts
screens.question: Among 60 students, 30 play sport. Of those 30, 15 also study music. What is the probability that a randomly selected sport player studies music?

steps.title: Identify the condition
steps.body: We are told the selected student plays sport, so restrict attention to the 30 sport players.

steps.title: Find the favourable count
steps.body: Within that group, 15 also study music.

screens.finalAnswer: The conditional probability is [[\frac12]], so the correct option is B.

screens.title: Statistics and Probability: key points
keyPoints: Choose representations according to whether data are categorical, discrete, continuous, or time-based.
Grouped means and graph-read quartiles are estimates.
Histogram frequency is class width times frequency density.
Correlation does not imply causation, and extrapolation is risky.
Add mutually exclusive routes, multiply successive branches, and restrict denominators for conditional probability.
reviewItems: Compare centre and spread using like-for-like statistics.
Use bar area—not height alone—in histograms.
Read quartiles at the correct cumulative positions.
Update probabilities when sampling without replacement.
Next lesson: Geometry and Data Exam-style Questions.

screens.question: The point [[(1,4)]] lies on [[y=f(x)]]. Which point lies on [[y=f(x+2)-3]]?

diagram.caption: Inside changes act horizontally; outside changes act vertically.

screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[f(x+2)]] moves the point left by 2, and subtracting 3 moves it down by 3: [[(1,4)\mapsto(-1,1)]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: A positive change inside f moves the graph left. The outside subtraction then lowers the y-coordinate.

screens.question: The point [[(4,-3)]] lies on [[y=f(x)]]. Where does it move on [[y=-f(2x)]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[f(2x)]] divides x-coordinates by 2, and the outside negative reflects y-values: [[(4,-3)\mapsto(2,3)]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Divide the x-coordinate by the inside multiplier, then negate the y-coordinate.

screens.question: Let [[f(x)=2x+1]] and [[g(x)=x^{2}]]. Find [[g(f(1))]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Evaluate the inner function f first, then apply g to its output.

screens.question: Which statement describes [[y=3(x-2)^{2}-4]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The vertex is [[(2,-4)]], and the positive coefficient means the parabola opens upwards.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Read the vertex from completed-square form and use the sign of the squared-term coefficient.

screens.question: The graphs [[y=x^{2}+1]] and [[y=3x-1]] intersect at two points. What is the sum of their x-coordinates?

diagram.caption: Equate the two expressions to find intersection coordinates.

screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[x^{2}+1=3x-1]] gives [[x^{2}-3x+2=0]], with roots 1 and 2. Their sum is 3.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Equate the two y-values, rearrange, and factorise the resulting quadratic.

screens.question: The quantity y is directly proportional to [[x^{3}]]. Given [[y=16]] when [[x=2]], find y when [[x=3]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Find k from the first pair of values before substituting the new x-value.

screens.question: The quantity y is inversely proportional to [[x^{2}]]. If x doubles, by what factor does y change?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[y=kx^{-2}]], so doubling x multiplies y by [[2^{-2}=\frac14]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Apply the inverse-square power to the scale factor: [[2^{-2}]].

screens.question: Similar solids have corresponding lengths in the ratio [[3:5]]. The smaller volume is 216. Find the larger volume.

diagram.caption: Volume scales with the cube of the length ratio.

screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The volume factor is [[\left(\frac53\right)^{3}=\frac{125}{27}]], and [[216\times\frac{125}{27}=1000]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Cube the length scale factor from smaller to larger before scaling the volume.

screens.question: Each interior angle of a regular polygon is 156°. How many sides does it have?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The exterior angle is [[180^{\circ}-156^{\circ}=24^{\circ}]], so the number of sides is [[\frac{360}{24}=15]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: First convert the interior angle to an exterior angle, then divide 360° by it.

screens.question: Which information is not sufficient in general to prove that two triangles are congruent?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. SSA can produce two different triangles, so it is not a general congruence criterion.
screens.incorrectFeedback: The standard criteria are SSS, SAS, ASA, and RHS. A non-included angle with two sides can be ambiguous.

screens.question: An enlargement with centre the origin and scale factor [[-\frac12]] maps [[(6,-4)]] to which point?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Multiply each coordinate by [[-\frac12]] to obtain [[(-3,2)]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: For a centre at the origin, multiply the position vector by the scale factor.

screens.question: A cuboid has side lengths 6, 8, and 24. Find its space diagonal.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Apply Pythagoras in three dimensions by adding the squares of all three perpendicular side lengths.

screens.question: One angle of a cyclic quadrilateral is 112°. What is the opposite angle?

diagram.caption: Opposite angles in a cyclic quadrilateral sum to 180°.

screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Opposite angles sum to 180°, so [[180^{\circ}-112^{\circ}=68^{\circ}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the cyclic-quadrilateral rule that opposite angles are supplementary.

screens.question: Points A and B have position vectors a and b. Which expression equals [[\overrightarrow{AB}]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The route from A to B is destination minus starting position: [[\mathbf{b}-\mathbf{a}]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Subtract the starting position vector from the destination position vector.

screens.question: Values in classes [[0\le x<10]] and [[10\le x<20]] have frequencies 4 and 6. Estimate the mean.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Using midpoints 5 and 15 gives [[\frac{4(5)+6(15)}{10}=\frac{110}{10}=11]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Multiply each class midpoint by its frequency, add, and divide by the total frequency.

screens.question: A histogram class has width 8 and frequency density 2.5. Find the class frequency.

diagram.caption: In a histogram, bar area equals frequency.

screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[8\times2.5=20]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Multiply the class width by its frequency density.

screens.question: A cumulative-frequency graph contains 120 observations. At which cumulative frequency should [[Q_{3}]] be read?

diagram.caption: The upper quartile lies three quarters of the way through the ordered data.

screens.incorrectFeedback: The upper quartile is at three quarters of the total frequency.

screens.question: A scatter graph shows strong negative correlation between X and Y. Which statement is justified?

diagram.caption: Correlation describes an association; it does not establish causation.

options: Larger X-values tend to be associated with smaller Y-values.
Increasing X necessarily causes Y to decrease.
Increasing Y necessarily causes X to decrease.
Every point must lie on the line of best fit.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Negative correlation means the variables tend to move in opposite directions. It does not establish a causal mechanism.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Describe the observed association without making a causal claim.

screens.question: A bag contains 4 green and 3 yellow counters. Two are drawn without replacement. What is the probability that both are green?

diagram.caption: Without replacement, the probabilities on the second stage change.

screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[\frac47\times\frac36=\frac{12}{42}=\frac27]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: After one green counter is removed, 3 green counters remain among 6 counters.

screens.question: Of 50 students, 20 study physics. Of those 20, 8 also study economics. Find [[P(\text{economics}\mid\text{physics})]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Restrict the sample space to the 20 physics students: [[\frac8{20}=\frac25]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: The condition is physics, so the denominator must be the number of physics students, not all 50 students.

screens.title: Geometry and Data: exam review
keyPoints: Track points through graph transformations and evaluate composite functions from the inside out.
Translate proportion into equations and apply squared or cubed scale factors where appropriate.
Combine angle, congruence, similarity, circle, coordinate, and vector facts carefully.
Interpret grouped data through midpoints, density, cumulative positions, and honest statements about correlation.
For probability, update dependent branches and restrict conditional denominators.
reviewItems: Redo transformation questions using point mappings.
Label the dimension of every similarity factor.
Draw or annotate geometry before calculating.
Use histogram area rather than height alone.
Repeat dependent and conditional probability questions until the denominator is automatic.

screens.title: A mathematical statement has a definite truth value
screens.body:
A statement is a claim that is either true or false. “[[7]] is prime” is true; “[[9]] is prime” is false. A question or an instruction is not a statement.

Compound statements use and, or, and not. In mathematics, “or” is inclusive: “A or B” allows A, B, or both to be true.
screens.keyPoint: “A and B” is true only when both parts are true. “A or B” is false only when both parts are false.

screens.title: Read compound claims one word at a time

steps.title: And requires both
steps.body: The claim “[[n]] is positive and even” requires both properties. A positive odd number does not satisfy it.

steps.title: Or includes the overlap
steps.body: The claim “[[n]] is divisible by [[2]] or [[3]]” includes multiples of [[6]], because both conditions may hold.

steps.title: Not reverses the truth value
steps.body: The negation of “[[x\gt4]]” is “[[x\le4]]”, not merely “[[x\lt4]]”. Equality must be included.

screens.question: Which integer satisfies “divisible by [[2]] or divisible by [[3]]” because both parts are true?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[6]] is divisible by both [[2]] and [[3]], and inclusive “or” allows this overlap.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Look for a number divisible by both [[2]] and [[3]], not exactly one of them.

screens.title: An if-then statement points in one direction
screens.body:
“If A, then B” promises that whenever A is true, B must also be true. A is the condition and B is the conclusion.

The claim says nothing about cases where A is false. It also does not automatically allow the direction to be reversed.
screens.keyPoint: To disprove “if A, then B”, find a case where A is true but B is false.

diagram.caption: The contrapositive reverses and negates both parts, preserving the original meaning.

screens.title: Small words decide the direction

steps.title: “A if B”
steps.body: This means: if B is true, then A is true. B is sufficient for A.

steps.title: “A only if B”
steps.body: This means: if A is true, then B must be true. B is necessary for A.

steps.title: “A if and only if B”
steps.body: Both directions must hold: if A then B, and if B then A.

screens.question: What does “an integer is divisible by [[4]] only if it is even” mean?
options: If an integer is even, it is divisible by [[4]].
If an integer is divisible by [[4]], it is even.
An integer is divisible by [[4]] exactly when it is odd.
No even integer is divisible by [[4]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. “A only if B” means if A, then B. Being even is necessary for divisibility by [[4]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: The words “only if” introduce a necessary condition: divisibility by [[4]] cannot occur without evenness.

screens.title: The converse is different; the contrapositive is equivalent
screens.body:
From “if A, then B”:

The converse is “if B, then A”. It may be true or false independently.

The contrapositive is “if not B, then not A”. It always has the same truth value as the original statement.
screens.keyPoint: Reversing alone gives the converse. Reversing and negating both parts gives the contrapositive.

diagram.caption: A valid implication always carries its contrapositive with it.

screens.title: Test each direction separately
screens.question: Consider: “If an integer is divisible by [[4]], then it is even.” Which related statement is guaranteed to be true?
options: If an integer is even, then it is divisible by [[4]].
If an integer is not even, then it is not divisible by [[4]].
If an integer is not divisible by [[4]], then it is not even.
Every even integer is divisible by [[8]].

steps.title: Identify the original direction
steps.body: Divisible by [[4]] is the condition; even is the conclusion.

steps.title: Check the converse
steps.body: The converse says every even integer is divisible by [[4]]. This is false: [[6]] is even but not divisible by [[4]].

steps.title: Form the contrapositive
steps.body: Reverse and negate both parts: if an integer is not even, then it is not divisible by [[4]]. This is equivalent to the original.

screens.finalAnswer: If an integer is not even, then it is not divisible by [[4]].

screens.title: You can now track the direction of an argument.
screens.body: Next, necessary and sufficient conditions make those directions precise.

screens.title: Sufficient guarantees; necessary is required
screens.body:
A is sufficient for B when A guarantees B. B is necessary for A when A cannot be true without B.

For example, being divisible by [[4]] is sufficient for being even. Being even is necessary for being divisible by [[4]], but it is not sufficient.
screens.keyPoint: In “if A, then B”, A is sufficient for B, while B is necessary for A.

diagram.caption: Every multiple of 4 is even, but not every even integer is a multiple of 4.

screens.question: For a quadrilateral to be a rectangle, being a square is:
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Every square is a rectangle, so being a square is sufficient. Many rectangles are not squares, so it is not necessary.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Ask both questions separately: does square guarantee rectangle, and must every rectangle be square?

screens.title: If and only if requires two proofs
screens.body:
To establish “A if and only if B”, prove both “if A, then B” and “if B, then A”. This makes each condition both necessary and sufficient for the other.

One direction may be easy while the reverse needs a different argument.
screens.keyPoint: Never claim equivalence after proving only one implication.

screens.question: Which statement is true for every integer [[n]]?
options: [[n]] is even if and only if [[n^{2}]] is even.
[[n]] is divisible by [[4]] if and only if [[n]] is even.
[[n]] is prime if and only if [[n]] is odd.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Even [[n]] gives even [[n^{2}]]. Conversely, if [[n]] were odd, [[n^{2}]] would be odd, so even [[n^{2}]] forces even [[n]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Test both directions and look for counterexamples such as negative integers, [[2]], or even numbers not divisible by [[4]].

screens.title: Logic of Arguments: key points
keyPoints: Mathematical “or” is inclusive.
“A only if B” means if A, then B.
The converse reverses an implication and need not be true.
The contrapositive reverses and negates both parts and is equivalent.
In “if A, then B”, A is sufficient and B is necessary.
reviewItems: Identify the condition and conclusion before reasoning.
Translate “if”, “only if”, and “if and only if” carefully.
Test a converse independently.
Use a counterexample where the condition holds and conclusion fails.
Prove both directions before claiming equivalence.

screens.title: “For all” and “for some” make different promises
screens.body:
A universal statement claims something for every object in its domain. An existential statement claims that at least one suitable object exists.

“For some” means “for at least one”; it does not mean “for several” or “for most”.
screens.keyPoint: The domain matters. “Every number has a square root” changes truth value depending on whether the domain is positive reals, all reals, or complex numbers.

diagram.caption: Universal claims cover every case; existential claims need at least one witness.

screens.title: Negation swaps the quantifier and reverses the property

steps.title: Negating “for all”
steps.body: The negation of “every object has property P” is “there exists at least one object that does not have property P”.

steps.title: Negating “there exists”
steps.body: The negation of “there exists an object with property P” is “every object fails to have property P”.

steps.title: Negating and/or
steps.body: The negation of “A and B” is “not A or not B”. The negation of “A or B” is “not A and not B”.

screens.question: What is the negation of “for every real [[x]], [[x^{2}\ge x]]”?
options: There exists a real [[x]] such that [[x^{2}\lt x]].
There exists a real [[x]] such that [[x^{2}\ge x]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Negating “for every” gives “there exists”, and negating [[x^{2}\ge x]] gives [[x^{2}\lt x]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: A universal claim fails when at least one counterexample exists. Reverse both the quantifier and the inequality.

screens.title: A direct proof is a justified chain
screens.body:
Begin with the assumptions, use definitions or known results, and derive the required conclusion. Each step must follow from something already established.

A proof is not a collection of true sentences; the sentences must be connected in the correct logical order.
screens.keyPoint: State why each deduction is valid, especially when dividing, taking roots, or applying a theorem with conditions.

diagram.caption: A valid proof connects the given information to the target without gaps.

screens.title: Translate definitions into algebra
screens.question: Prove directly that the sum of two odd integers is even.

steps.title: Use the definition of odd
steps.body: Let the odd integers be [[2a+1]] and [[2b+1]], where [[a]] and [[b]] are integers.

steps.title: Apply the definition of even
steps.body: Since [[a+b+1]] is an integer, the sum is [[2]] times an integer and is therefore even.

screens.finalAnswer: The sum is [[2(a+b+1)]], so it is even.

screens.question: Suppose [[n]] is an integer divisible by [[6]]. Which deduction proves directly that [[n]] is divisible by [[3]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Since [[2k]] is an integer, [[n=3(2k)]] matches the definition of divisibility by [[3]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the definition of divisibility and express [[n]] as [[3]] times an integer.

screens.title: Cases must be exhaustive
screens.body:
Proof by cases divides the problem into possibilities that cover the whole domain. Prove the result separately in each case.

For integers, even and odd form an exhaustive pair: every integer is exactly one of them.
screens.keyPoint: Listing several examples is not proof by cases. Each case must represent an entire class.

diagram.caption: Once both exhaustive branches are proved, no possibility remains.

screens.title: Prove the result on every branch
screens.question: Prove that [[n^{2}+n]] is even for every integer [[n]].

steps.title: Conclude exhaustively
steps.body: Every integer is even or odd, so the result holds for every integer [[n]].

screens.finalAnswer: [[n^{2}+n]] is even in both exhaustive cases.

screens.title: You can construct direct proofs and proofs by cases.
screens.body: Now use contradiction when denying the conclusion exposes an impossibility.

screens.title: Assume the opposite and force an impossibility
screens.body:
To prove a claim by contradiction, assume its negation. Deduce consequences until they conflict with the assumptions, a known fact, or each other.

The contradiction shows that the temporary assumption was impossible, so the original claim must hold.
screens.keyPoint: State the negation precisely. A vague “suppose the result is false” often hides the real logical work.

screens.title: A reduced fraction cannot have two even parts
screens.question: Which assumption begins a proof by contradiction that [[\sqrt2]] is irrational?

steps.title: Assume the negation in lowest terms
steps.body: Suppose [[\sqrt2=\frac ab]], where [[a]] and [[b]] are coprime positive integers.

steps.title: Force a to be even
steps.body: Squaring gives [[a^{2}=2b^{2}]], so [[a^{2}]] is even and therefore [[a]] is even. Write [[a=2k]].

steps.title: Force b to be even
steps.body: Then [[4k^{2}=2b^{2}]], so [[b^{2}=2k^{2}]] and [[b]] is even. This contradicts [[a]] and [[b]] being coprime.

screens.finalAnswer: Assume [[\sqrt2=\frac ab]] in lowest terms, then derive a common factor of [[2]].

screens.title: Small cases suggest a pattern but do not prove it
screens.body:
Examples are valuable for discovering a conjecture and testing its plausibility. A proof must then cover every case in the stated domain.

Even a million successful examples cannot establish a universal claim; the next case could fail.
screens.keyPoint: Use examples to discover and challenge a conjecture, then replace the pattern with a general argument.

diagram.caption: Successful tests build confidence, but one later failure can overturn a universal conjecture.

screens.question: Which is the strongest direct reason that [[n^{2}-n]] is even for every integer [[n]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Of two consecutive integers, one is even, so their product is even for every integer [[n]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Choose the statement that covers an arbitrary integer rather than a finite list of examples.

screens.title: Methods of Proof and Quantifiers: key points
keyPoints: Negating “for all” produces “there exists a counterexample”.
Negating “there exists” produces a universal non-existence claim.
Direct proof builds a justified chain from assumptions to conclusion.
Proof by cases requires exhaustive cases.
Contradiction assumes the precise negation and derives an impossibility.
reviewItems: State the domain and quantifier.
Translate definitions into algebra.
Explain every deduction in a proof chain.
Distinguish representative cases from isolated examples.
Use small cases to form conjectures, never as universal proof.

screens.title: One valid failure defeats a universal claim
screens.body:
To disprove “every object has property P”, find one object in the stated domain that does not have property P.

A counterexample must satisfy every assumption in the claim and fail exactly the conclusion being asserted.
screens.keyPoint: A counterexample disproves a universal statement. It does not prove a different universal statement.

diagram.caption: Any single failure is enough to show that “all” was too strong.

screens.title: Attack the weakest point of the claim

steps.title: Read the domain
steps.body: Integers, positive reals, and non-zero reals allow different examples. A value outside the domain proves nothing.

steps.title: Try structural edge cases
steps.body: Test [[0]], [[1]], negative values, equality cases, repeated roots, and the smallest prime or composite numbers.

steps.title: Preserve the premise
steps.body: For “if A, then B”, the example must make A true and B false. If A is false, the implication has not been challenged.

screens.question: Which values disprove the claim “if [[ab=ac]], then [[b=c]]” for all real [[a,b,c]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Both products equal [[0]], so the premise holds, but [[1\ne2]], so the conclusion fails.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Make the common factor [[a]] equal to [[0]], while choosing different values for [[b]] and [[c]].

screens.title: Cancellation is division, so zero must be excluded
screens.body:
From [[ab=ac]], moving to [[b=c]] divides both sides by [[a]]. This is valid only when [[a\ne0]].

Many false proofs hide an undefined operation: division by zero, a square root of a negative real, or a denominator that may vanish.
screens.keyPoint: Before dividing by an expression, state or establish that it is non-zero.

screens.title: Locate the first invalid step
screens.question: A purported proof starts with [[a=b\ne0]] and concludes [[2=1]]. Where does it fail?

steps.title: Follow the valid algebra
steps.body: From [[a=b]], multiplying by [[a]] gives [[a^{2}=ab]]. Subtracting [[b^{2}]] gives [[a^{2}-b^{2}=ab-b^{2}]].

steps.title: Expose the illegal division
steps.body: Cancelling [[a-b]] divides by it. But [[a=b]], so [[a-b=0]]. Division by zero creates the false conclusion.

screens.finalAnswer: The cancellation of [[a-b=0]] is invalid.

screens.question: Which pair disproves “if [[\sin A=\sin B]], then [[A=B]]” for angles between [[0^{\circ}]] and [[180^{\circ}]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[\sin30^{\circ}=\sin150^{\circ}=\frac12]], but the angles are unequal.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use supplementary angles, which have equal sine values.

screens.title: You can disprove claims and expose illegal cancellation.
screens.body: Next, diagnose arguments whose individual facts are true but whose logic is invalid.

screens.title: A true conclusion does not repair a broken argument
screens.body:
An argument is valid only when each conclusion follows from its premises. Common failures include assuming the converse, assuming the desired result, using an example as a proof, or applying a theorem without its conditions.

A proof may reach a true statement through invalid reasoning.
screens.keyPoint: Judge the connection between steps, not only whether each isolated sentence looks true.

diagram.caption: One unsupported arrow breaks the proof chain.

screens.title: Ask what information each operation loses

steps.title: Taking square roots
steps.body: From [[x^{2}=9]], both [[x=3]] and [[x=-3]] are possible. Writing only the positive root loses a solution.

steps.title: Squaring both sides
steps.body: Squaring is not reversible without checking. It can introduce extraneous solutions because opposite numbers have the same square.

steps.title: Dividing by a variable expression
steps.body: Dividing by [[x]] discards the possibility [[x=0]]. Separate the zero case before division.

steps.title: Checking examples
steps.body: Examples can refute a universal claim, but successful examples cannot prove it.

screens.title: Proving the reverse direction is not enough
screens.question: A student tries to prove: “if [[n^{2}]] is even, then [[n]] is even.” They show that even [[n]] gives even [[n^{2}]]. What is wrong?
options: They proved the converse, not the required implication.
Even integers do not have even squares.

steps.title: Name the target
steps.body: The required direction starts from “[[n^{2}]] is even” and must conclude “[[n]] is even”.

steps.title: Name what was actually proved
steps.body: The student's argument starts from even [[n]] and concludes even [[n^{2}]]. That is the converse.

steps.title: Repair with the contrapositive
steps.body: If [[n]] is odd, write [[n=2k+1]]. Then [[n^{2}=2(2k^{2}+2k)+1]], which is odd. Therefore even [[n^{2}]] forces even [[n]].

screens.finalAnswer: The proof establishes the converse; the contrapositive proves the required direction.

screens.question: A student solves [[x(x-3)=0]] by dividing by [[x]] and obtains only [[x=3]]. Which solution was lost?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Dividing by [[x]] assumes [[x\ne0]], so it discards the valid solution [[x=0]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Substitute the value that makes the divided factor [[x]] equal to zero.

screens.title: Match the evidence to the quantifier
screens.body:
To prove a universal claim, give a general argument covering every case. To disprove it, give one counterexample.

To prove an existential claim, one explicit witness is enough. To disprove it, show that no object in the domain can work.
screens.keyPoint: The same example can prove “there exists” but only test “for all”.

diagram.caption: The quantifier determines how much evidence the argument needs.

screens.question: Which counterexample disproves “every prime number is odd”?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. [[2]] is prime and even, so it satisfies the premise and fails the conclusion.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Choose a number that is genuinely prime but not odd.

screens.title: Disproving Statements: key points
keyPoints: A counterexample must lie in the domain and satisfy the premise.
One counterexample disproves a universal claim.
Cancellation requires the cancelled expression to be non-zero.
Equal squares or equal sine values do not force equal inputs.
Proving a converse does not prove the original implication.
reviewItems: Test zero, boundary values, negatives, and smallest cases.
Find the first invalid line of a purported proof.
Check for lost or extraneous solutions after non-reversible operations.
Distinguish examples from general arguments.
Repair invalid implications using a direct proof or contrapositive.

screens.question: In mathematical logic, when is “A or B” false?
options: Only when both A and B are false
When both A and B are true
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Mathematical “or” is inclusive, so it is true when either or both parts are true.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Inclusive “or” permits both parts to be true.

screens.question: The statement “A if B” means:
options: A and B are both false
A if and only if B
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. “A if B” says that B is sufficient for A.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Read the phrase as “A happens if B happens”.

screens.question: The statement “A only if B” means:
options: A and B cannot both hold
B is sufficient but not necessary for A
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. B is a necessary condition for A, so A cannot hold without B.
screens.incorrectFeedback: The words “only if” introduce the condition that must be present whenever A is true.

screens.question: What is the converse of “if an integer is divisible by [[6]], then it is even”?
options: If an integer is even, then it is divisible by [[6]].
If an integer is not even, then it is not divisible by [[6]].
If an integer is not divisible by [[6]], then it is not even.
Every integer divisible by [[6]] is even.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The converse reverses the condition and conclusion without negating them.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Reverse the two parts but do not negate either one.

screens.question: What is the contrapositive of “if an integer is divisible by [[6]], then it is even”?
options: If an integer is even, then it is divisible by [[6]].
If an integer is not even, then it is not divisible by [[6]].
If an integer is not divisible by [[6]], then it is not even.
If an integer is odd, then it is divisible by [[6]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Reverse the parts and negate both. This statement is equivalent to the original.
screens.incorrectFeedback: A contrapositive both reverses and negates the condition and conclusion.

screens.question: For an integer to be divisible by [[3]], being divisible by [[6]] is:
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Divisibility by [[6]] guarantees divisibility by [[3]], but numbers such as [[9]] show it is not necessary.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Ask whether every multiple of [[6]] is a multiple of [[3]], then whether every multiple of [[3]] must be a multiple of [[6]].

screens.question: Which statement is true for every integer [[n]]?
options: [[n]] is divisible by [[6]] if and only if it is divisible by both [[2]] and [[3]].
[[n]] is even if and only if it is divisible by [[4]].
[[n]] is odd if and only if it is prime.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Divisibility by [[6]] gives divisibility by [[2]] and [[3]], and the two coprime factors together give divisibility by [[6]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Test both directions, including negative integers and small values such as [[2]] and [[6]].

screens.question: What is the negation of “for every integer [[n]], [[n^{2}\ge0]]”?
options: There exists an integer [[n]] such that [[n^{2}\lt0]].
There exists an integer [[n]] such that [[n^{2}\ge0]].
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Negating a universal statement produces the existence of at least one failure.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Replace “for every” with “there exists” and negate the inequality.

screens.question: What is the negation of “there exists an even prime greater than [[2]]”?
options: No even prime is greater than [[2]].
Every integer greater than [[2]] is composite.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Negating existence says that no object in the domain has all the stated properties.
screens.incorrectFeedback: The negation must rule out every even prime greater than [[2]], not merely produce a different example.

screens.question: What is the negation of “[[n]] is positive and even”?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. To make “A and B” false, at least one part must fail, giving “not A or not B”.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Negating a conjunction changes “and” to inclusive “or”.

screens.question: Suppose integers [[a]] and [[b]] are both divisible by [[5]]. Which line proves directly that [[a+b]] is divisible by [[5]]?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Since [[r+s]] is an integer, the sum is [[5]] times an integer.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Translate both divisibility assumptions into algebra using arbitrary integers.

screens.question: Which observation proves [[n(n+1)]] is even for every integer [[n]]?

diagram.caption: Every integer is even or odd, and consecutive integers have opposite parity.

options: One of two consecutive integers [[n,n+1]] is even.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. In every case, the product contains an even factor.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the exhaustive parity relationship between consecutive integers.

screens.question: To prove by contradiction that there is no greatest integer, what should be assumed first?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Assume the negation of the target. Then [[N+1]] is an integer greater than [[N]], producing the contradiction.
screens.incorrectFeedback: A contradiction proof begins by assuming the precise opposite of the statement being proved.

screens.question: Which value disproves “[[x^{2}\gt x]] for every real [[x]]”?

diagram.caption: A universal inequality fails if one real input violates it.

screens.correctFeedback: Correct. At [[x=0]], the claim becomes [[0\gt0]], which is false.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Test a boundary value where the two sides become equal.

screens.question: Which missing condition makes the deduction “[[ab=ac]], therefore [[b=c]]” valid?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Cancelling [[a]] divides by it, which requires [[a\ne0]].
screens.incorrectFeedback: Identify the expression used as a divisor during cancellation.

screens.question: Why is “[[\sin A=\sin B]], therefore [[A=B]]” invalid without further restrictions?
options: Supplementary angles can have equal sine values.
Every angle has a different sine value.
Sine is defined only for acute angles.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. For example, [[\sin30^{\circ}=\sin150^{\circ}]], although the angles differ.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Recall the symmetry of the sine graph between quadrants I and II.

screens.question: Squaring [[\sqrt{x+2}=x]] gives candidates [[x=2]] and [[x=-1]]. Which candidate is extraneous?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Substitution gives [[\sqrt1=1\ne-1]], so [[x=-1]] solves the squared equation but not the original.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Substitute each candidate into the original unsquared equation.

screens.question:
To prove that the square of an even integer is even, which order is logically correct?

1. Therefore [[n^{2}]] is even.
2. Let [[n=2k]], where [[k]] is an integer.
3. Then [[n^{2}=4k^{2}=2(2k^{2})]].
4. [[2k^{2}]] is an integer.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Begin with the definition, calculate, establish the remaining factor is an integer, then conclude evenness.
screens.incorrectFeedback: A proof must begin from the assumption and place the conclusion only after its justification.

screens.question: A conjecture has been checked successfully for the first [[100]] positive integers. What follows?
options: The conjecture is proved for every positive integer.
The conjecture is plausible but still needs a general proof.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Finite evidence can suggest a universal claim but cannot establish every remaining case.
screens.incorrectFeedback: A universal statement requires a general argument, regardless of how many examples have been tested.

screens.question: Suppose: if [[x-2]] is a factor of [[f(x)]], then [[f(2)=0]]. Also, [[f(2)=0]] only if [[c=4]]. Given [[c\ne4]], what must follow?

diagram.caption: Chain the implications, then use the contrapositive.

screens.correctFeedback: Correct. If [[x-2]] were a factor, then [[f(2)=0]], which would require [[c=4]]. Since [[c\ne4]], the factor is impossible.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Follow both implications forward, then apply their combined contrapositive to [[c\ne4]].

screens.title: Logic and Proof: exam review
keyPoints: Translate implication language before attempting deductions.
Use contrapositives, converse checks, and necessary/sufficient conditions precisely.
Negate quantifiers and compound statements completely.
Choose direct proof, exhaustive cases, contradiction, or counterexample appropriately.
Audit every algebraic operation and every arrow in a purported proof.
reviewItems: Do not use formal truth tables; reason from the meaning of the words.
State domains and hidden non-zero conditions.
Check whether an argument proves the required direction.
Substitute candidates after non-reversible operations.
Replace evidence from examples with a general proof.
